> **Final KTU Takeaway:** The EOQ model is mathematically elegant, practically robust, and almost always appears as a 14-mark problem in the KTU Module-4 ESE. Master the Wilson Formula, the equi-cost property, and the $T^*$ & $R$ conversions — that alone secures 80% of the marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventory Classification and Its Use in Controlling Inventory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT862 - OPERATIONAL RESEARCH AND INDUSTRIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inventory Classification and Its Use in Controlling Inventory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inventory Classification** is the systematic process of categorizing inventory items into distinct groups based on selected criteria (such as annual consumption value, criticality, procurement difficulty, or usage rate) so that **differentiated inventory control policies** can be applied to each group. It operationalizes the management principle that *not all items deserve equal managerial attention or control effort*.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT862)**, inventory classification is treated as the **first and most critical step** of scientific inventory control, because it enables managers to convert a chaotic list of thousands of SKUs into a manageable, prioritized framework.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Wardrobe Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)**
>
> Imagine opening your overflowing wardrobe. You do **not** spend the same amount of care on every piece of clothing:
> - Your **expensive silk saree** (used rarely but very costly) gets wrapped in muslin cloth and stored carefully.
> - Your **everyday cotton T-shirts** (used daily, cheap) are just folded and stacked.
> - Your **old worn-out jeans** (rarely used) sit in a corner box.
>
> **Inventory classification works exactly like this.** Instead of giving every inventory item the same level of attention, you *sort* items into buckets (A, B, C, Vital, Essential, Fast-moving, etc.) and **control each bucket differently**. This saves time, money, and reduces stockouts.

### 1.3 Why Classification Is Needed

Standard metrics and universal constants that drive this topic:

- **Pareto's 80/20 Principle** — roughly **80%** of the total inventory value is contributed by only about **20%** of the items.
- **ABC Cut-off Thresholds** — A items: top **70–80%** of value; B items: next **15–25%**; C items: remaining **5–10%**.
- **Stockout Cost vs. Carrying Cost** — these two costs move in **opposite directions**, so classification helps find the optimum balance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The module specifically requires the student to understand *classification systems* (ABC, HML, SDE, FSN, VED, GOLF, SOS) and *how each system aids in controlling inventory*. Memorizing at least the **ABC, VED, FSN, and SDE** classifications is essential for ESE.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Inventory Classification Systems

A structured inventory may be sliced from several independent angles. The KTU syllabus emphasizes the following eight systems:

#### A. ABC Analysis (Always Better Control)
- Based on **Annual Consumption Value (ACV)** of each item.
- ACV = Annual Usage Quantity × Unit Price.
- Pareto-driven: vital few vs. trivial many.

| Class | % of Items | % of Annual Value | Control Effort |
|:-----:|:----------:|:-----------------:|:--------------:|
| **A** | 10–20%     | 70–80%            | Tight, frequent review |
| **B** | 20–30%     | 15–25%            | Moderate review      |
| **C** | 50–70%     | 5–10%             | Loose, simple controls |

#### B. HML Analysis (High, Medium, Low)
- Based on **Unit Price** of the item (not annual value).
- **H** items → high unit price → high security, low stock.
- **L** items → low unit price → bulk stock allowed.

#### C. SDE Analysis (Scarce, Difficult, Easy)
- Based on **Procurement / Sourcing Difficulty**.
- **S** → scarce, long lead time → maintain buffer stock.
- **E** → easily available → just-in-time ordering.

#### D. FSN Analysis (Fast, Slow, Non-moving)
- Based on **Issue / Usage Rate** from stores.
- **F** → fast-moving → high reorder frequency.
- **N** → non-moving → consider disposal / write-off.

#### E. VED Analysis (Vital, Essential, Desirable)
- Based on **Criticality to Production / Operations**.
- **V** → production stops without it → never run out.
- **D** → wanted but not critical → low safety stock.

#### F. GOLF Analysis (Government, Ordinary, Local, Foreign)
- Based on **Source of Supply**.

#### G. SOS Analysis (Seasonal, Off-Seasonal)
- Based on **Demand Pattern** over the year.

#### H. XYZ Analysis
- Based on **Closing Inventory Value** stored at year-end (used in retail).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Classification — Core Logic Steps

1. **Identify the objective** of control (cost? continuity? cash?).
2. **Pick the right classification axis** (value, criticality, movement).
3. **Compute the metric** for every SKU (e.g., ACV for ABC).
4. **Rank items in descending order** of the chosen metric.
5. **Cumulative-percentage plot** (Pareto curve).
6. **Apply cut-off percentages** to assign A/B/C classes.
7. **Map each class to a control policy** (reorder level, review period, safety stock).
8. **Audit periodically** because demand and prices shift.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in every ESE numerical on this topic.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Meaning / Units |
|:-:|:--------------------|:----------:|:----------------|
| 1 | Annual Consumption Value | $ACV_i = Q_i \times P_i$ | $Q_i$ = annual quantity (units), $P_i$ = unit price (₹) |
| 2 | % of Total Value | $\%V_i = \dfrac{ACV_i}{\sum_j ACV_j} \times 100$ | Share of annual value |
| 3 | Cumulative % Value | $\%CV_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \%V_i$ | Running total from top |
| 4 | Cumulative % of Items | $\%N_k = \dfrac{k}{n} \times 100$ | $k$ = item rank, $n$ = total items |
| 5 | A-class boundary | $\%CV \le 70\text{–}80\%$ | Tight control zone |
| 6 | B-class boundary | $70\text{–}80\% < \%CV \le 90\text{–}95\%$ | Moderate control zone |
| 7 | C-class boundary | $\%CV > 90\text{–}95\%$ | Loose control zone |
| 8 | Total Inventory Investment | $TIV = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i \times P_i)$ | Sum of ACVs (₹) |
| 9 | EOQ (linked concept) | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D C_o}{C_h}}$ | Order quantity for each class |

> **Engineering Utility:** ABC classification drives **warehouse slotting**, **cycle-count frequency**, **safety-stock allocation**, and even **ERP system priority flags** in industries ranging from automotive assembly lines to pharmaceutical cold-chain logistics.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — ABC Analysis (Board-Style Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> The following is a typical 7–10 mark ESE numerical. **Practice writing every column explicitly.**

**Problem Statement (KTU-pattern):**
A manufacturing firm maintains 10 inventory items with the following data. Perform **ABC analysis** and classify the items.

| Item | Annual Usage (units) | Unit Price (₹) |
|:----:|:-------------------:|:--------------:|
| 1    | 50                  | 200            |
| 2    | 100                 | 50             |
| 3    | 500                 | 10             |
| 4    | 200                 | 80             |
| 5    | 1,000               | 5              |
| 6    | 60                  | 300            |
| 7    | 20                  | 500            |
| 8    | 400                 | 20             |
| 9    | 300                 | 30             |
| 10   | 150                 | 60             |

### Step 1 — Compute Annual Consumption Value (ACV) for each item

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACV_1 &= 50 \times 200 = 10{,}000 \\
ACV_2 &= 100 \times 50 = 5{,}000 \\
ACV_3 &= 500 \times 10 = 5{,}000 \\
ACV_4 &= 200 \times 80 = 16{,}000 \\
ACV_5 &= 1{,}000 \times 5 = 5{,}000 \\
ACV_6 &= 60 \times 300 = 18{,}000 \\
ACV_7 &= 20 \times 500 = 10{,}000 \\
ACV_8 &= 400 \times 20 = 8{,}000 \\
ACV_9 &= 300 \times 30 = 9{,}000 \\
ACV_{10} &= 150 \times 60 = 9{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Total Inventory Value (TIV)

$$
\begin{aligned}
TIV &= \sum_{i=1}^{10} ACV_i \\
&= 10{,}000 + 5{,}000 + 5{,}000 + 16{,}000 + 5{,}000 \\
&\quad + 18{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 9{,}000 + 9{,}000 \\
&= 95{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute % Value Contribution of each item

$$
\%V_i = \frac{ACV_i}{TIV} \times 100
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%V_1 &= \tfrac{10{,}000}{95{,}000} \times 100 = 10.526\% \\
\%V_2 &= \tfrac{5{,}000}{95{,}000} \times 100 = 5.263\% \\
\%V_3 &= \tfrac{5{,}000}{95{,}000} \times 100 = 5.263\% \\
\%V_4 &= \tfrac{16{,}000}{95{,}000} \times 100 = 16.842\% \\
\%V_5 &= \tfrac{5{,}000}{95{,}000} \times 100 = 5.263\% \\
\%V_6 &= \tfrac{18{,}000}{95{,}000} \times 100 = 18.947\% \\
\%V_7 &= \tfrac{10{,}000}{95{,}000} \times 100 = 10.526\% \\
\%V_8 &= \tfrac{8{,}000}{95{,}000} \times 100 = 8.421\% \\
\%V_9 &= \tfrac{9{,}000}{95{,}000} \times 100 = 9.474\% \\
\%V_{10} &= \tfrac{9{,}000}{95{,}000} \times 100 = 9.474\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Rank items in descending order of ACV

| Rank | Item | ACV (₹) | % Value | Cumulative % Value | Cumulative % of Items |
|:----:|:----:|:-------:|:-------:|:------------------:|:----------------------:|
| 1    | 6    | 18,000  | 18.947  | 18.947             | 10.0                   |
| 2    | 4    | 16,000  | 16.842  | 35.789             | 20.0                   |
| 3    | 1    | 10,000  | 10.526  | 46.316             | 30.0                   |
| 4    | 7    | 10,000  | 10.526  | 56.842             | 40.0                   |
| 5    | 9    | 9,000   | 9.474   | 66.316             | 50.0                   |
| 6    | 10   | 9,000   | 9.474   | 75.789             | 60.0                   |
| 7    | 8    | 8,000   | 8.421   | 84.211             | 70.0                   |
| 8    | 2    | 5,000   | 5.263   | 89.474             | 80.0                   |
| 9    | 3    | 5,000   | 5.263   | 94.737             | 90.0                   |
| 10   | 5    | 5,000   | 5.263   | 100.000            | 100.0                  |

### Step 5 — Apply ABC cut-off thresholds (standard KTU values)

- **A class** → items contributing up to ~70% of cumulative value.
- **B class** → next 20% of cumulative value (i.e., 70–90%).
- **C class** → remaining items (90–100%).

### Step 6 — Final Classification

| Class | Items | Cumulative % Value | Cumulative % of Items |
|:-----:|:-----:|:------------------:|:----------------------:|
| **A** | 6, 4, 1, 7, 9 | 66.32% | 50% |
| **B** | 10, 8        | 84.21% | 70% |
| **C** | 2, 3, 5      | 100.00%| 100% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Only **5 out of 10 items (A-class)** consume about **two-thirds** of inventory investment — these need *tight control*. The **C-class items** (2, 3, 5) together account for only **~16%** of value and may be ordered in bulk with minimal follow-up.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Inventory Classification Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Raw Inventory List of n Items]) --> dataColl[Collect Annual Quantity and Unit Price]
    dataColl --> acvCalc[Compute ACV = Q times P for Each Item]
    acvCalc --> tivSum[Sum all ACVs to get Total Inventory Value]
    tivSum --> pctCalc[Compute percent Value Contribution]
    pctCalc --> rankSort[Rank Items in Descending Order]
    rankSort --> cumCalc[Compute Cumulative percent Value]
    cumCalc --> curvePlot[Plot Pareto Curve]
    curvePlot --> classDecide{Apply Cutoff Thresholds}
    classDecide --> aClass[A Class: up to 70 percent Value]
    classDecide --> bClass[B Class: 70 to 90 percent Value]
    classDecide --> cClass[C Class: above 90 percent Value]
    aClass --> policyA[Tight Control, Frequent Review, Low Safety Stock]
    bClass --> policyB[Moderate Control, Periodic Review]
    cClass --> policyC[Loose Control, Bulk Ordering, High Safety Stock]
    policyA --> audit[Periodic Audit and Reclassification]
    policyB --> audit
    policyC --> audit
    audit --> loopEnd((Re-enter Classification Cycle))
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Multi-Dimensional Classification Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph axis1[Axis 1 ABC Value]
        abcA[A Class Vital Few]
        abcB[B Class]
        abcC[C Class Trivial Many]
    end
    subgraph axis2[Axis 2 VED Criticality]
        vedV[Vital]
        vedE[Essential]
        vedD[Desirable]
    end
    subgraph axis3[Axis 3 FSN Movement]
        fsnF[Fast Moving]
        fsnS[Slow Moving]
        fsnN[Non Moving]
    end
    itemCenter([Each SKU]) --> axis1
    itemCenter --> axis2
    itemCenter --> axis3
    abcA --> policyOut[Dual or Triple Control Policy]
    vedV --> policyOut
    fsnF --> policyOut
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** A single SKU may simultaneously be **A-V-F** (high value, vital, fast-moving) — requiring *the tightest* combined control — or **C-D-N** (cheap, desirable, non-moving) — warranting *write-off decisions*. This **matrix intersection logic** is a frequent KTU Part-B question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define ABC analysis of inventory. State the basis of classification.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> ABC analysis, also known as **Always Better Control**, is an inventory classification technique based on the **Annual Consumption Value (ACV)** of each item. Items are ranked in descending order of ACV and grouped into three classes — **A** (about 10–20% of items consuming 70–80% of value), **B** (20–30% of items consuming 15–25% of value), and **C** (50–70% of items consuming only 5–10% of value). It is grounded in the **Pareto 80/20 principle**.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for basis, 1 mark for thresholds.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between FSN and VED classification of inventory.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **FSN classification** is based on the **rate of issue / usage** from stores — items are labelled **F** (Fast), **S** (Slow), or **N** (Non-moving). It focuses on *movement velocity*.
>
> **VED classification** is based on the **criticality of an item to production** — items are labelled **V** (Vital — production halts without it), **E** (Essential — production seriously affected), or **D** (Desirable — production continues but with inconvenience). It focuses on *operational criticality*.

*(Award 1½ marks each for the distinctions.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

A company uses 8 items with the following data:

| Item | Annual Demand (units) | Unit Cost (₹) |
|:----:|:---------------------:|:-------------:|
| 1    | 400                   | 12            |
| 2    | 80                    | 100           |
| 3    | 2,000                 | 4             |
| 4    | 600                   | 25            |
| 5    | 50                    | 250           |
| 6    | 1,500                 | 8             |
| 7    | 100                   | 80            |
| 8    | 300                   | 30            |

**(a)** Perform ABC analysis and classify the items. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Suggest a suitable inventory control policy for each class. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Step-by-step ABC Computation**

**Step 1: Compute Annual Consumption Value (ACV)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACV_1 &= 400 \times 12 = 4{,}800 \\
ACV_2 &= 80 \times 100 = 8{,}000 \\
ACV_3 &= 2{,}000 \times 4 = 8{,}000 \\
ACV_4 &= 600 \times 25 = 15{,}000 \\
ACV_5 &= 50 \times 250 = 12{,}500 \\
ACV_6 &= 1{,}500 \times 8 = 12{,}000 \\
ACV_7 &= 100 \times 80 = 8{,}000 \\
ACV_8 &= 300 \times 30 = 9{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total Inventory Value**

$$
TIV = 4{,}800 + 8{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 12{,}500 + 12{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 9{,}000 = 77{,}300 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3: Rank, % Value, Cumulative % Value**

| Rank | Item | ACV (₹) | % Value | Cum % Value | Cum % Items |
|:----:|:----:|:-------:|:-------:|:-----------:|:-----------:|
| 1    | 4    | 15,000  | 19.40   | 19.40       | 12.5        |
| 2    | 5    | 12,500  | 16.17   | 35.57       | 25.0        |
| 3    | 6    | 12,000  | 15.52   | 51.10       | 37.5        |
| 4    | 8    | 9,000   | 11.64   | 62.74       | 50.0        |
| 5    | 2    | 8,000   | 10.35   | 73.09       | 62.5        |
| 6    | 3    | 8,000   | 10.35   | 83.44       | 75.0        |
| 7    | 7    | 8,000   | 10.35   | 93.79       | 87.5        |
| 8    | 1    | 4,800   | 6.21    | 100.00      | 100.0       |

**Step 4: ABC Classification (using standard cut-offs)**

- **A class** (≤ 70% cumulative value) → Items 4, 5, 6, 8, 2
- **B class** (70–90% cumulative value) → Items 3, 7
- **C class** (> 90% cumulative value) → Item 1

**Part (b) — Inventory Control Policy per Class**

| Class | Items | Recommended Policy |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-------------------|
| **A** | 4, 5, 6, 8, 2 | Tight control; continuous (perpetual) review; small safety stock; rigorous forecasting; close supplier tie-ups; frequent cycle counts. |
| **B** | 3, 7 | Moderate control; periodic review (e.g., weekly or fortnightly); moderate safety stock; combined ordering for some items. |
| **C** | 1 | Loose control; large bulk orders to avail quantity discounts; high safety stock allowed; annual or half-yearly review. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert**
> - Forgetting to **rank in descending order** before computing cumulative % (common 2-mark deduction).
> - Using **wrong cut-off thresholds** (writing A = 50% instead of the value-based ~70%).
> - Not mentioning **continuous vs. periodic review** distinction in Part (b) — KTU specifically awards marks for naming the *review system*.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the various methods of inventory classification with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss how classification aids in **controlling inventory** in a manufacturing organization. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Inventory Classification Methods**

| # | Method | Basis | Example |
|:-:|:------:|:------|:--------|
| 1 | **ABC** | Annual consumption value (₹) | ₹50,000 bearings vs. ₹200 stationery clips |
| 2 | **HML** | Unit price of item | Diamond tool bits vs. paper clips |
| 3 | **SDE** | Procurement difficulty | Imported IC chip vs. local nut-bolt |
| 4 | **FSN** | Issue/usage rate from stores | Daily consumed fasteners vs. decade-old spares |
| 5 | **VED** | Criticality to production | Aircraft landing-gear valve vs. office paint |
| 6 | **GOLF** | Source of supply | Local vendor vs. imported OEM |
| 7 | **SOS** | Seasonal nature of demand | Air-conditioner parts vs. year-round cables |
| 8 | **XYZ** | Closing inventory value | High-end retail stock at year-end |

*Examiners expect at least **5–6 methods** with the basis clearly stated for each. (7 marks)*

**Part (b) — How Classification Aids Inventory Control**

1. **Selective Attention** — Managers can focus effort on the A-class (vital few) rather than spreading themselves thin across hundreds of C-class items.
2. **Cost Reduction** — Carrying cost, ordering cost, and stockout cost are managed differently for each class, leading to lower *Total Inventory Cost (TIC)*.
3. **Improved Service Level** — V-class and A-class items get high safety stock, drastically reducing the chance of production-stopping stockouts.
4. **Better Working Capital** — Money is *not* locked up in slow-moving C-class items; cash flow improves.
5. **Optimum Safety Stock** — High safety stock for A/V items, low for C/D items — minimizes inventory investment.
6. **Efficient Warehouse Layout** — A-class items placed near the issue counter for fast access; C-class items placed at the back.
7. **Cycle Counting** — A-class items counted weekly, B-class monthly, C-class quarterly — saving labour.
8. **Supplier Management** — Long-term contracts for A/S items; competitive bidding for C/E items.

*(7 marks — minimum 5 well-explained points required.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Question B)**
> - Listing classification methods **without stating the basis** loses ½ mark per row.
> - Part (b) answers that are *purely theoretical* without linking to *manufacturing decisions* (e.g., reorder level, safety stock, layout) get only 4/7.
> - Avoid using vague phrases like *"inventory becomes easy"* — use precise terms: *selective control, dual EOQ policy, differential safety stock.*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must remember before ESE):**

- ☐ ABC = **Annual Consumption Value**; basis = ₹ value (Pareto).
- ☐ HML = **Unit Price**; basis = ₹ per piece.
- ☐ SDE = **Procurement / Sourcing Difficulty**.
- ☐ FSN = **Issue / Movement Rate** from stores.
- ☐ VED = **Criticality to Production / Operations**.
- ☐ GOLF = **Source of Supply**; SOS = **Seasonal Demand**.
- ☐ A-class items → ~10–20% of SKUs but ~70–80% of ₹ value.
- ☐ A-class control → **continuous review + low safety stock + tight forecasting**.
- ☐ C-class control → **periodic review + bulk orders + high safety stock**.
- ☐ Formula $ACV_i = Q_i \times P_i$ and cumulative % ranking must always be in **descending** order.
- ☐ **Triple-classification matrices** (e.g., A-V-F vs. C-D-N) combine multiple criteria for *finest-grained* control — favourite KTU Part-B question.
- ☐ Classification must be **periodically audited** — reclassify at least once per year because prices and demand shift.
- ☐ Pareto's 80/20 principle is the *philosophical backbone* of ABC analysis — quote it explicitly in definitions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deterministic Inventory Models: Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT862 - OPERATIONAL RESEARCH AND INDUSTRIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deterministic Inventory Models: Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deterministic Inventory Model:** A mathematical inventory control model in which the demand rate is **known with certainty (constant or variable but predictable)** and is not subject to probabilistic variation. The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) model is the foundational deterministic inventory model, introduced by **Ford W. Harris (1915)** and later formalized by **R.H. Wilson (1934)**.

**Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** is the **optimal order quantity (Q\*)** that minimizes the **Total Inventory Cost (TC)** — comprising **Ordering Cost**, **Holding (Carrying) Cost**, and **Purchase Cost** — under the assumption of **constant demand rate**, **instantaneous replenishment**, and **no stockouts**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme High-Yield Terminology**
> - **Demand Rate (D):** Annual/periodic demand in units. Treated as **deterministic** in this module.
> - **Order Quantity (Q):** The lot size ordered each time inventory is replenished.
> - **Lead Time (L):** Time between placing an order and receiving it. Assumed **constant** and **known**.
> - **Reorder Point (ROP):** The inventory level at which a new order must be placed: $ROP = D \times L$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Corner Bakery" Intuition

Imagine you run a small bakery that sells **5,000 loaves of bread every year** (demand is fixed — no randomness). Every time you call your supplier, they charge a delivery fee (ordering cost), and storing unsold bread in your shop costs rent + refrigeration (holding cost). If you order **too frequently in small batches**, delivery fees pile up. If you order **rarely in massive batches**, you waste money storing bread. The **EOQ is the magical sweet spot** — the order size where these two opposing costs balance perfectly, giving you the **lowest total yearly cost**.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Mental Hook for Exams:**
> *Order frequently → Ordering cost dominates. Order rarely → Holding cost dominates. EOQ = the U-shaped Total Cost curve's minimum.*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Symbols (KTU Convention)

The following symbols are **standard across all KTU textbooks and exam papers**:

- **D** = Annual demand (units/year)
- **Q** = Order quantity per cycle (units)
- **N** = Number of orders per year (dimensionless)
- **Co** = Ordering cost per order (₹/order)
- **Ch** = Holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)
- **P** = Purchase cost per unit (₹/unit)
- **t\*\*** = Optimal cycle time
- **TC** = Total annual inventory cost
- **TBO** = Time Between Orders = cycle time

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Assumptions of the Classical EOQ Model (Harris-Wilson Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandatory Assumptions — Memorize These Verbatim:**
> 1. Demand rate **D is constant, known, and deterministic**.
> 2. Lead time **L is constant and known**.
> 3. Order is received **instantaneously** (infinite replenishment rate).
> 4. **No stockouts** are permitted — inventory is always positive.
> 5. Ordering cost **Co is fixed per order**, independent of Q.
> 6. Holding cost **Ch is linear** in average inventory.
> 7. Purchase cost per unit **P is constant** (no quantity discounts).
> 8. Order quantity **Q is constant** for every cycle.

## 2.2 Cost Components — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### (A) Purchase Cost (PC)
- **Why:** You pay the supplier for the goods themselves.
- **How:** Since D is fixed and P is constant, PC is **fixed regardless of Q** and is often ignored during optimization.
- **Math:** $PC = P \cdot D$

### (B) Ordering Cost (OC) — *Also called Setup Cost*
- **Why:** Each order incurs paperwork, transportation, inspection, and administrative expenses.
- **How:** If you place $\frac{D}{Q}$ orders per year at cost Co each:
- **Math:** $OC = \left(\dfrac{D}{Q}\right) \cdot C_o$
- **Behavior:** **Decreases** as Q increases (fewer orders).

### (C) Holding Cost (HC) — *Also called Carrying Cost*
- **Why:** Inventory sitting in the warehouse costs rent, insurance, depreciation, obsolescence, and capital opportunity cost.
- **How:** With instantaneous replenishment, average inventory is $\frac{Q}{2}$.
- **Math:** $HC = \left(\dfrac{Q}{2}\right) \cdot C_h$
- **Behavior:** **Increases** as Q increases.

### (D) Shortage Cost (SC)
- **Why:** In the basic EOQ model, this is **assumed to be ZERO**.
- **Math:** $SC = 0$ (basic model) — generalized in **EOQ with shortages**.

## 2.3 The Fundamental Trade-Off

| Cost Type | Function of Q | Shape |
|-----------|---------------|-------|
| Ordering Cost | $\frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q}$ | Hyperbola (decreasing) |
| Holding Cost | $\frac{C_h \cdot Q}{2}$ | Straight line (increasing) |
| Purchase Cost | $P \cdot D$ | Horizontal line (constant) |
| **Total Cost** | $\frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q} + \frac{C_h \cdot Q}{2} + P \cdot D$ | **U-shaped convex curve** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **the single most-examined component** of this topic. Memorize every row.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Optimal Value |
|---|----------|---------|------|---------------|
| 1 | Annual Ordering Cost | $OC = \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot C_o$ | ₹/year | — |
| 2 | Annual Holding Cost | $HC = \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot C_h$ | ₹/year | — |
| 3 | Annual Purchase Cost | $PC = P \cdot D$ | ₹/year | — |
| 4 | Total Annual Cost | $TC = \dfrac{D \cdot C_o}{Q} + \dfrac{Q \cdot C_h}{2} + P \cdot D$ | ₹/year | Minimum |
| 5 | **EOQ (Optimal Q)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot C_o}{C_h}}$ | units | $\sqrt{2DC_o/C_h}$ |
| 6 | Optimal Cycle Time | $t^* = \dfrac{Q^*}{D} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot C_o}{D \cdot C_h}}$ | year | — |
| 7 | Number of Orders/Year | $N^* = \dfrac{D}{Q^*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{D \cdot C_h}{2 \cdot C_o}}$ | orders | — |
| 8 | Reorder Point | $ROP = D \cdot L$ | units | — |
| 9 | Min Total Variable Cost | $TC_v^* = \sqrt{2 \cdot D \cdot C_o \cdot C_h}$ | ₹/year | (excluding PC) |
| 10 | Optimal Avg. Inventory | $\dfrac{Q^*}{2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{D \cdot C_o}{2 \cdot C_h}}$ | units | — |
| 11 | Max Inventory Level | $Q^*$ (since no shortages) | units | — |
| 12 | Time Between Orders | $TBO = \dfrac{1}{N^*}$ | year | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Observation:** At the optimum $Q^*$, Ordering Cost **equals** Holding Cost:
> $$\frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^*} = \frac{Q^* \cdot C_h}{2}$$
> This is the **fundamental EOQ equilibrium condition** that examiners love to test.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Industrial Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Batch production planning, raw material procurement.
- **Retail & Supply Chain:** Walmart, Amazon, Reliance Retail use EOQ-derived principles for stock replenishment.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Vaccine and drug inventory planning.
- **Automotive:** Spare parts inventory for service centers.
- **Aerospace:** Critical component stocking where deterministic demand is valid.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EOQ Formula

### Step 1 — Set Up the Total Cost Function

The total annual inventory cost is the sum of all three cost components:

$$
TC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q}}_{\text{Ordering Cost}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q \cdot C_h}{2}}_{\text{Holding Cost}} + \underbrace{P \cdot D}_{\text{Purchase Cost}}
$$

**Explanation:** Every year we place $\frac{D}{Q}$ orders at cost Co each; we carry an average inventory of $\frac{Q}{2}$ (since inventory ramps linearly from 0 to Q and drops back to 0); and we purchase D units at price P each.

### Step 2 — Note That Purchase Cost is Constant

Since $P \cdot D$ does **not** depend on Q, it has no effect on the optimization. We can minimize only the **variable cost**:

$$
TC_v(Q) = \frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot C_h}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Take the First Derivative With Respect to Q

$$
\frac{d\,TC_v(Q)}{d\,Q} = -\frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^2} + \frac{C_h}{2}
$$

**Explanation:** Derivative of $\frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q}$ is $-\frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^2}$; derivative of $\frac{Q \cdot C_h}{2}$ is $\frac{C_h}{2}$.

### Step 4 — Apply the First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC)

For a minimum, set the derivative to zero:

$$
-\frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^2} + \frac{C_h}{2} = 0
$$

### Step 5 — Solve Algebraically for Q

$$
\frac{C_h}{2} = \frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^2}
$$

$$
Q^2 = \frac{2 \cdot D \cdot C_o}{C_h}
$$

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot D \cdot C_o}{C_h}}
$$

### Step 6 — Verify It's a Minimum (Second-Order Condition)

$$
\frac{d^2\,TC_v(Q)}{d\,Q^2} = \frac{2 \cdot D \cdot C_o}{Q^3}
$$

At $Q > 0$, this is **strictly positive** $\Rightarrow$ the function is **convex** $\Rightarrow$ the critical point is a **global minimum**. ✓

### Step 7 — Derive the Minimum Total Variable Cost

Substitute $Q^*$ back into $TC_v$:

$$
TC_v^* = \frac{D \cdot C_o}{\sqrt{2DC_o/C_h}} + \frac{C_h}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2DC_o}{C_h}}
$$

$$
TC_v^* = \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot C_o \cdot C_h}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot C_o \cdot C_h}{2}} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot C_o \cdot C_h}{2}}
$$

$$
TC_v^* = \sqrt{2 \cdot D \cdot C_o \cdot C_h}
$$

### Step 8 — Optimal Number of Orders Per Year

$$
N^* = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{D}{\sqrt{2DC_o/C_h}} = \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot C_h}{2 \cdot C_o}}
$$

### Step 9 — Optimal Cycle Length

$$
t^* = \frac{1}{N^*} = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot C_o}{D \cdot C_h}} \quad \text{(years)}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Comprehensive Problem)

### Problem Statement
> A manufacturing firm requires **10,000 units/year** of a raw material. The ordering cost is **₹200 per order**, and the holding cost is **₹10 per unit per year**. The purchase price is **₹50 per unit**. Lead time is **6 days** (assume 360-day year).
>
> **Determine:**
> (a) Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)
> (b) Optimal number of orders per year
> (c) Optimal cycle time
> (d) Total annual inventory cost
> (e) Reorder point

### Given Data
- $D = 10{,}000$ units/year
- $C_o = ₹200$/order
- $C_h = ₹10$/unit/year
- $P = ₹50$/unit
- $L = 6$ days, Year = 360 days

### (a) Economic Order Quantity

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot D \cdot C_o}{C_h}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200}{10}} = \sqrt{\frac{4{,}000{,}000}{10}} = \sqrt{400{,}000}
$$

$$
Q^* = 632.46 \approx 632 \text{ units (or } 633 \text{ units)}
$$

### (b) Optimal Number of Orders per Year

$$
N^* = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{10{,}000}{632.46} = 15.81 \approx 16 \text{ orders/year}
$$

### (c) Optimal Cycle Time

$$
t^* = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{632.46}{10{,}000} = 0.06325 \text{ years}
$$

Converting to days: $t^* = 0.06325 \times 360 = 22.77$ days per cycle.

### (d) Total Annual Inventory Cost

$$
OC = \frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^*} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 200}{632.46} = ₹3{,}162.28
$$

$$
HC = \frac{Q^* \cdot C_h}{2} = \frac{632.46 \times 10}{2} = ₹3{,}162.28
$$

$$
PC = P \cdot D = 50 \times 10{,}000 = ₹5{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
TC = OC + HC + PC = 3{,}162.28 + 3{,}162.28 + 5{,}00{,}000 = ₹5{,}06{,}324.56
$$

### (e) Reorder Point

Daily demand rate: $d = \frac{D}{360} = \frac{10{,}000}{360} = 27.78$ units/day

$$
ROP = d \times L = 27.78 \times 6 = 166.67 \approx 167 \text{ units}
$$

### Verification of Equilibrium Condition
At optimum, $OC = HC = ₹3{,}162.28$ ✓ — this confirms the EOQ is correctly calculated.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the EOQ Decision System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data: D, Co, Ch, P, L] --> B[Compute EOQ using sqrt 2DCo/Ch]
    B --> C{Is Q positive and feasible?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Calculate N and t star]
    C -- No --> E[Adjust Parameters and Recompute]
    D --> F[Compute TC star minimum]
    F --> G[Compute Reorder Point D times L]
    G --> H[Output EOQ Decision: Order Q star units every t star period]
    H --> I[Monitor Inventory Level]
    I --> J{Inventory equals ROP?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Place New Order Q star]
    J -- No --> L[Continue Consumption]
    K --> I
    L --> I
```

## 4.2 Inventory Level vs Time (Sawtooth Pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Start Inventory Q star] --> P2[Linear Decrease to 0]
    P2 --> P3[Instantaneous Replenishment to Q star]
    P3 --> P4[Linear Decrease to 0]
    P4 --> P5[Instantaneous Replenishment to Q star]
    P5 --> P6[Continues Cyclically]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The sawtooth rises **instantaneously** to $Q^*$ when an order arrives, then decreases **linearly** at rate D (constant demand), reaching **0** exactly when the next order arrives — under basic EOQ assumptions.

## 4.3 Cost Behavior Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Total Cost Function] --> oc[Ordering Cost D times Co divided by Q]
    start --> hc[Holding Cost Q times Ch divided by 2]
    start --> pc[Purchase Cost P times D Constant]
    oc --> shape1[Hyperbolic Decreasing Curve]
    hc --> shape2[Linear Increasing Line]
    shape1 --> min[Sum gives U shaped Total Cost]
    shape2 --> min
    min --> eoq[Minimum at Q star where OC equals HC]
    pc --> min
```

## 4.4 Reorder Point System Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inventory at Q star] --> B[Consumption at Rate D per year]
    B --> C[Inventory Drops Linearly]
    C --> D{Level equals ROP equals D times L}
    D -- Triggered --> E[Place Order of Q star units]
    E --> F[Lead Time L days elapse]
    F --> G[Order Arrives, Inventory Resets to Q star]
    G --> B
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Economic Order Quantity (EOQ). State any four assumptions of the classical EOQ model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** EOQ is the optimal order quantity that minimizes the total annual inventory cost (sum of ordering, holding, and purchase costs) under conditions of constant deterministic demand, instantaneous replenishment, and zero stockouts. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot D \cdot C_o}{C_h}}
$$

**Four Assumptions (1.5 marks):**
1. Demand rate D is **constant, known, and deterministic**.
2. Lead time L is **constant and known**.
3. Replenishment is **instantaneous** (infinite rate).
4. **No stockouts** are allowed — inventory is always non-negative.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between Ordering Cost and Holding Cost with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Ordering Cost | Holding Cost |
|--------|---------------|--------------|
| **Nature** | Fixed per order | Variable with average inventory |
| **Formula** | $\frac{D}{Q} \cdot C_o$ | $\frac{Q}{2} \cdot C_h$ |
| **Behavior vs Q** | Decreases as Q increases | Increases as Q increases |
| **Examples** | Documentation, transport, inspection | Rent, insurance, obsolescence, depreciation |
| **Minimization** | Maximize Q | Minimize Q |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice:** Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

A company uses **40,000 units/year** of an item. The ordering cost is **₹500 per order** and holding cost is **₹8 per unit per year**. The purchase cost is **₹20 per unit** and lead time is **one month** (assume 12 months/year).

**Solve the following:**

**(a) [7 marks]** Determine the EOQ, number of orders per year, and optimal time between orders.

**(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the total annual inventory cost and reorder point.

---

### **Solution to Question A**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 40{,}000$ units/year
- $C_o = ₹500$/order
- $C_h = ₹8$/unit/year
- $P = ₹20$/unit
- $L = 1$ month = $\frac{1}{12}$ year

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Calculate EOQ**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot D \cdot C_o}{C_h}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 40{,}000 \times 500}{8}} = \sqrt{\frac{40{,}000{,}000}{8}} = \sqrt{5{,}000{,}000}
$$

$$
Q^* = 2236.07 \approx 2236 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2: Number of Orders per Year**

$$
N^* = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{40{,}000}{2236.07} = 17.89 \approx 18 \text{ orders/year}
$$

**Step 3: Optimal Time Between Orders**

$$
t^* = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{2236.07}{40{,}000} = 0.0559 \text{ year} \approx 0.671 \text{ months} \approx 20.13 \text{ days}
$$

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 4: Total Annual Inventory Cost Components**

$$
OC = \frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^*} = \frac{40{,}000 \times 500}{2236.07} = ₹8{,}944.27
$$

$$
HC = \frac{Q^* \cdot C_h}{2} = \frac{2236.07 \times 8}{2} = ₹8{,}944.27
$$

$$
PC = P \cdot D = 20 \times 40{,}000 = ₹8{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
TC = 8{,}944.27 + 8{,}944.27 + 8{,}00{,}000 = ₹8{,}17{,}888.54
$$

**Step 5: Reorder Point**

Monthly demand: $d_m = \frac{D}{12} = \frac{40{,}000}{12} = 3333.33$ units/month

$$
ROP = d_m \times L = 3333.33 \times 1 = 3333.33 \approx 3334 \text{ units}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

A retailer sells **5,000 units/year** of a product. The cost of placing an order is **₹100** and the carrying cost is **₹5 per unit per year**. The unit price is **₹40** and lead time is **15 days** (assume 365-day year).

**Solve the following:**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compute EOQ, optimal cycle length in days, and minimum total variable inventory cost.

**(b) [7 marks]** If the manager chooses an order quantity of **1,000 units** instead of EOQ, find the **extra cost incurred per year**. Comment on the result.

---

### **Solution to Question B**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 5{,}000$ units/year
- $C_o = ₹100$/order
- $C_h = ₹5$/unit/year
- $P = ₹40$/unit
- $L = 15$ days, Year = 365 days

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Compute EOQ**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot D \cdot C_o}{C_h}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 100}{5}} = \sqrt{\frac{1{,}000{,}000}{5}} = \sqrt{200{,}000}
$$

$$
Q^* = 447.21 \approx 447 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2: Optimal Cycle Length in Days**

$$
t^* = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{447.21}{5{,}000} = 0.0894 \text{ year}
$$

Converting: $t^* = 0.0894 \times 365 = 32.65$ days per cycle.

**Step 3: Minimum Total Variable Cost**

$$
TC_v^* = \sqrt{2 \cdot D \cdot C_o \cdot C_h} = \sqrt{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 100 \times 5} = \sqrt{5{,}000{,}000}
$$

$$
TC_v^* = ₹2236.07/\text{year}
$$

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 4: Total Variable Cost at Q = 1000 units**

$$
TC_v(1000) = \frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot C_h}{2} = \frac{5{,}000 \times 100}{1000} + \frac{1000 \times 5}{2}
$$

$$
TC_v(1000) = 500 + 2500 = ₹3000/\text{year}
$$

**Step 5: Extra Cost Incurred**

$$
\Delta TC = TC_v(1000) - TC_v^* = 3000 - 2236.07 = ₹763.93/\text{year}
$$

**Comment:** Since 1000 > 447 (EOQ), the firm is **over-ordering**, leading to **excessive holding cost (₹2500 vs ₹1118)** that outweighs the saving in ordering cost. The annual penalty of **₹763.93** quantifies the loss from deviating from the optimal policy.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU EOQ Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting Purchase Cost** — Always include $P \cdot D$ in the final TC unless the question explicitly asks for *variable* cost only.
> 2. **Wrong unit conversion for Lead Time** — If L is given in days but D is annual, convert D to daily rate using $d = D/365$ (or D/360 as specified).
> 3. **Not rounding Q properly** — KTU accepts nearest integer; do NOT truncate EOQ to lower integer without recalculating TC at the truncated value.
> 4. **Forgetting the equilibrium condition** — Always verify: at $Q^*$, $OC = HC$. Examiners often award bonus marks for this verification.
> 5. **Confusing $C_o$ with per-unit ordering cost** — $C_o$ is the **total cost of placing ONE order**, not per unit.
> 6. **Missing the +1/−1 boundary check** when verifying minimization: substitute $Q^* \pm 1$ or $Q^*/2$, $2Q^*$ and ensure TC is higher.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ EOQ applies only when demand is **deterministic, constant, and known**.
- ✅ Core formula: $Q^* = \sqrt{2DC_o/C_h}$ — derived by setting $\frac{dTC}{dQ} = 0$.
- ✅ Total Cost: $TC = \frac{DC_o}{Q} + \frac{QC_h}{2} + PD$.
- ✅ At EOQ: **Ordering Cost = Holding Cost** (this is the equilibrium).
- ✅ Minimum Variable Cost: $TC_v^* = \sqrt{2DC_oC_h}$.
- ✅ Optimal Cycle Time: $t^* = Q^*/D$.
- ✅ Number of orders: $N^* = D/Q^* = \sqrt{DC_h/2C_o}$.
- ✅ Reorder Point: $ROP = D \cdot L$ (independent of Q).
- ✅ Lead time given in days → use daily demand rate $d = D/365$.
- ✅ Average inventory under EOQ = $Q^*/2$ (since inventory drops linearly from Q to 0).
- ✅ Maximum inventory level = $Q^*$ (no safety stock in basic model).
- ✅ Basic EOQ = **no shortages**, **no quantity discounts**, **no production rate constraints** — these are extensions covered in subsequent topics.
- ✅ Always perform **second-derivative test** to confirm minimum.
- ✅ When deviating from EOQ, the extra cost is computed as $TC(Q) - TC(Q^*)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EOQ with Finite Supply
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT862 - OPERATIONAL RESEARCH AND INDUSTRIAL MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** EOQ with Finite Supply ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Economic Order Quantity with Finite Supply (EPQ / Production Lot Size Model)** is a deterministic inventory model in which replenishment is **not instantaneous** but occurs at a **finite production rate** $p$ (units per unit time), while demand is consumed continuously at a rate $d$ (units per unit time), with the binding physical constraint $p > d$. The objective is to determine the optimal lot size $Q^{*}$ that minimizes the sum of ordering (set-up) cost and inventory holding cost per unit time, given that inventory accumulates at the **net rate** $(p-d)$ during the production run and depletes at rate $d$ once production halts. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Note:** This model is also referred to as the **Economic Production Quantity (EPQ) Model** or the **Production Order Quantity Model**. It is the realistic generalization of the classical Wilson EOQ, which assumes instantaneous replenishment (i.e., $p \to \infty$). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners Imagine a bakery that bakes bread at a rate of **200 loaves/hour**, but customers buy bread continuously at **150 loaves/hour**. The bakery cannot bake the entire weekly demand in one shot — the ovens have a finite capacity. So they bake in **batches**. - While baking is ON, the display counter fills up at a net rate of $(200 - 150) = 50$ loaves/hour (production minus sale). - When the desired maximum counter level is reached, the baker **switches off the oven**. - From that point, the counter stock depletes purely because of customer purchases at $150$ loaves/hour. - When stock hits zero, the next batch production starts. The "switch on / switch off" cycle defines a **production cycle**, and the batch size baked per cycle is the **finite-supply EOQ** ($Q^{*}$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standardized KTU Symbols (use these verbatim in answer scripts):** > - $D$ — Annual demand (units/year) > - $p$ — Production rate (units/year), with $\mathbf{p > d}$ > - $d$ — Demand rate (units/year), often equal to $D$ > - $C_{o}$ — Ordering / set-up cost per order (Rs./order) > - $C_{h}$ — Holding cost per unit per year (Rs./unit/year) > - $Q$ — Lot size (units/order) > - $t_{p}$ — Production run time (time to produce one lot) > - $T$ — Cycle time (time between consecutive order placements) > - $I_{max}$ — Maximum inventory level ### 1.3 Key Assumptions (KTU Board-Standard Listing) 1. Demand rate $d$ is **known, constant, and uniform** over time. 2. Production rate $p$ is **finite, known, and constant**, with $p > d$. 3. Lot is produced in a **single batch** at rate $p$. 4. **Lead time is zero** (or constant and known — handled separately). 5. **No shortages are permitted** (no backlogging). 6. $C_{o}$, $C_{h}$, $p$, $d$ are constants; no quantity discounts. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Logic Steps of the Production-Inventory Cycle The inventory profile over one cycle is **trapezoidal in shape** (and reduces to a triangle only when production is instantaneous, $p \to \infty$, the classical EOQ case). 1. **Phase 1 — Production ON (duration $t_{p}$):** Inventory rises linearly from $0$ to $I_{max}$ at net rate $(p - d)$. 2. **Phase 2 — Production OFF (duration $T - t_{p}$):** Inventory falls linearly from $I_{max}$ to $0$ at rate $d$ (pure consumption). 3. **Cycle repeats** after $T$, the cycle time. ### 2.2 Mathematical Build-up of Cost Functions **Step 1 — Production time to manufacture one lot of size $Q$:** $$t_{p} = \frac{Q}{p}$$ **Step 2 — Maximum inventory reached at end of production run:** $$I_{max} = (p - d) \cdot t_{p} = (p - d) \cdot \frac{Q}{p} = Q \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Cycle time (one order placed every $T$ units of time):** $$T = \frac{Q}{d}$$ **Step 4 — Average inventory over a cycle:** Since the inventory is a triangle of height $I_{max}$ and base $T$: $$\text{Average Inventory} = \frac{I_{max}}{2} = \frac{Q}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Annual ordering cost** (number of orders per year $= D/Q$): $$OC = C_{o} \cdot \frac{D}{Q}$$ **Step 6 — Annual holding cost:** $$HC = C_{h} \cdot \frac{Q}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$ **Step 7 — Total annual variable cost:** $$TC(Q) = C_{o}\frac{D}{Q} + C_{h}\frac{Q}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The factor $\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)$ is the **Finite-Supply Correction Factor**. When $p \to \infty$ (instantaneous replenishment), this factor $\to 1$, and the formula reduces exactly to classical Wilson EOQ. This is the most-tested KTU concept in this module. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---| | Optimal Lot Size $Q^{*}$ | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_{o} D}{C_{h}\left(1 - d/p\right)}}$ | Units / order | | Equivalent form of $Q^{*}$ | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_{o} D p}{C_{h}(p - d)}}$ | Avoids fraction inside root | | Cycle Time $T^{*}$ | $T^{*} = \dfrac{Q^{*}}{D}$ | Years (or days) | | Production Run Time $t_{p}^{*}$ | $t_{p}^{*} = \dfrac{Q^{*}}{p}$ | Years (or days) | | Maximum Inventory $I_{max}$ | $I_{max} = Q^{*}\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)$ | Units | | Average Inventory $\bar{I}$ | $\bar{I} = \dfrac{I_{max}}{2}$ | Units | | Minimum Total Cost $TC^{*}$ | $TC^{*} = \sqrt{2 C_{o} D C_{h}\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)}$ | Rs. / year | | Number of orders per year $N^{*}$ | $N^{*} = \dfrac{D}{Q^{*}}$ | Orders / year | | Optimal Ordering Interval $T_{o}^{*}$ | $T_{o}^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_{o}}{C_{h} D}\cdot\dfrac{1}{1 - d/p}}$ | Years | > [!NOTE] > All costs must be expressed in **consistent time units** (e.g., Rs./year). If $d$ and $p$ are given per day, multiply by working days to get annual values, or use days consistently throughout. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Manufacturing industries** producing bolts, IC chips, packaged goods, where the machine cannot produce the entire annual demand in a single instant. - **Pharmaceutical batch production** with finite reactor capacity. - **Just-In-Time (JIT)** and lean production planning — to size Kanban batches. - **Aggregate production planning** in Industry 4.0 smart factories. - **Capacity planning** — the ratio $d/p$ is the **capacity utilization factor**; the model tells the planner how much idle capacity to allow. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Optimal Lot Size $Q^{*}$ Starting from the Total Cost function: $$TC(Q) = C_{o}\cdot\frac{D}{Q} + C_{h}\cdot\frac{Q}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$ **Step 1:** Differentiate $TC(Q)$ with respect to $Q$ to obtain the marginal cost. $$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{C_{o}D}{Q^{2}} + \frac{C_{h}}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Apply the first-order necessary condition for an unconstrained minimum (KTU Board Standard: set the first derivative to zero and verify second-order condition). $$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -\frac{C_{o}D}{Q^{2}} + \frac{C_{h}}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) = 0$$ **Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate the $Q^{2}$ term on one side. $$\frac{C_{h}}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) = \frac{C_{o}D}{Q^{2}}$$ **Step 4:** Cross-multiply to express $Q^{2}$. $$Q^{2}\cdot C_{h}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) = 2 C_{o} D$$ **Step 5:** Divide and take the positive square root (lot size is non-negative). $$Q^{2} = \frac{2 C_{o} D}{C_{h}\left(1 - d/p\right)}$$ $$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 C_{o} D}{C_{h}\left(1 - d/p\right)}}$$ **Step 6:** Verify the second-order condition — that this is a **minimum**, not a maximum. $$\frac{d^{2}(TC)}{dQ^{2}} = \frac{2 C_{o}D}{Q^{3}} \;>\; 0 \;\;\text{for all } Q > 0$$ Hence the critical point is a **strict local (and global) minimum**. $\blacksquare$ ### 3.2 Derivation of the Minimum Total Cost $TC^{*}$ **Step 1:** Substitute $Q = Q^{*}$ back into the total cost expression. $$TC^{*} = \frac{C_{o}D}{Q^{*}} + \frac{C_{h}Q^{*}}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$ **Step 2:** At the optimum, the two cost components are **equal** (a classical result). $$\frac{C_{o}D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{C_{h}Q^{*}}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Therefore, total cost is simply **twice either component**. $$TC^{*} = 2 \cdot \frac{C_{o}D}{Q^{*}}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute the value of $Q^{*}$ and simplify. $$TC^{*} = 2 C_{o} D \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C_{h}\left(1 - d/p\right)}{2 C_{o} D}} = \sqrt{2 C_{o} D C_{h}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)}$$ $$TC^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 C_{o} D C_{h}(p - d)}{p}}$$ ### 3.3 Fully Worked Numerical Example (Model Solution) > **[KTU University Exam — Module 4 Typical Pattern]** > A firm produces a component at a rate of **30,000 units/year**. The demand rate is **20,000 units/year**, ordering cost is **Rs. 200 per order**, and holding cost is **Rs. 4 per unit per year**. > Determine: (i) Economic Lot Size, (ii) Cycle time, (iii) Production run time, (iv) Maximum inventory, (v) Minimum total cost. **Given Data:** $$D = 20{,}000 \text{ units/yr}, \quad p = 30{,}000 \text{ units/yr}, \quad C_{o} = \text{Rs. } 200, \quad C_{h} = \text{Rs. } 4/\text{unit/yr}$$ **Compute the finite-supply correction factor:** $$1 - \frac{d}{p} = 1 - \frac{20{,}000}{30{,}000} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}$$ **(i) Economic Lot Size:** $$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 200 \cdot 20{,}000}{4 \cdot (1/3)}} = \sqrt{\frac{8{,}000{,}000}{4/3}} = \sqrt{6{,}000{,}000}$$ $$Q^{*} = 2449.49 \approx 2449 \text{ units}$$ **(ii) Cycle Time:** $$T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \frac{2449.49}{20{,}000} = 0.1225 \text{ years} \approx 44.7 \text{ days}$$ **(iii) Production Run Time:** $$t_{p}^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{p} = \frac{2449.49}{30{,}000} = 0.0816 \text{ years} \approx 29.8 \text{ days}$$ **(iv) Maximum Inventory:** $$I_{max} = Q^{*}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) = 2449.49 \times \frac{1}{3} = 816.50 \text{ units}$$ **(v) Minimum Total Cost:** $$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 200 \cdot 20{,}000 \cdot 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3}} = \sqrt{\frac{32{,}000{,}000}{3}} = \sqrt{10{,}666{,}666.67}$$ $$TC^{*} \approx \text{Rs. } 3266.0 \text{ per year}$$ **Verification (both components must match at optimum):** $$OC = \frac{C_{o} D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{200 \times 20{,}000}{2449.49} = \text{Rs. } 1632.99$$ $$HC = \frac{C_{h}Q^{*}}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) = \frac{4 \times 2449.49}{2} \times \frac{1}{3} = \text{Rs. } 1632.99$$ $$TC = OC + HC = \text{Rs. } 3265.98 \approx 3266 \;\checkmark$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Inventory Level vs. Time Profile (Finite Supply) The following Mermaid **state-flow diagram** captures the two-phase production-depletion cycle. (A true time-series plot is not directly supported in Mermaid, so a structured block-level functional flow is given instead.) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start of Cycle: Inventory = 0 units"] --> B["Production ON Phase"] B["Production ON Phase
Duration tp = Q by p
Net build rate = p minus d"] --> C["Maximum Inventory I max reached
I max = Q times 1 minus d by p"] C --> D["Production OFF Phase"] D["Production OFF Phase
Duration T minus tp
Pure depletion at rate d"] --> E["Inventory returns to 0 units"] E --> F{"Cycle time T complete?
T = Q by d"} F -- Yes --> A F -- No --> D ``` **Interpretation of the cycle:** - The block **"Production ON Phase"** corresponds to the rising edge of the inventory curve (slope $= p - d$). - The block **"Production OFF Phase"** corresponds to the falling edge (slope $= -d$). - The trapezoidal profile collapses to a **triangle** when $p \to \infty$ (instantaneous replenishment, the classical Wilson EOQ case). ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the EPQ Model ```mermaid flowchart LR I1["Input Parameters
D, p, Co, Ch"] --> P1["Compute Finite-Supply
Factor 1 minus d by p"] P1 --> P2["Compute Optimal
Lot Size Q star"] P2 --> O1["Output Q star"] P2 --> P3["Compute Cycle Time
T star = Q star by D"] P2 --> P4["Compute Production
Time tp star = Q star by p"] P2 --> P5["Compute Max Inventory
I max = Q star times 1 minus d by p"] P2 --> P6["Compute Min Total Cost
TC star = sqrt of 2 Co D Ch times 1 minus d by p"] P3 --> O2["Output T star"] P4 --> O3["Output tp star"] P5 --> O4["Output I max"] P6 --> O5["Output TC star"] ``` > [!NOTE] > This block diagram is the **standard KTU teaching artifact** for the EPQ model. In the exam, students are expected to draw (or describe) the trapezoidal inventory profile and label the four key points: $0$, $I_{max}$ (peak), $T$ (cycle length), and $t_{p}$ (production sub-interval). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]** Define the **EOQ with finite supply model**. State its key assumptions. **Model Answer:** The **EOQ with finite supply (EPQ)** is a deterministic inventory model in which the lot is replenished gradually at a finite production rate $p$ (units/year) rather than instantaneously, while demand is consumed continuously at rate $d$ (units/year), with $p > d$. It determines the **optimal lot size $Q^{*}$** that minimizes the sum of ordering cost and holding cost per unit time. **Key assumptions:** 1. Demand rate $d$ is **known and constant**. 2. Production rate $p$ is **finite and constant**, with $p > d$. 3. **No shortages** are permitted. 4. **Lead time is zero** (or constant). 5. Ordering cost $C_{o}$ and holding cost $C_{h}$ are **known and constant**. 6. Replenishment occurs in a **single batch** at rate $p$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]** **Distinguish between the classical EOQ model and the EOQ with finite supply model** in terms of replenishment, inventory profile shape, and cost formula. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Classical EOQ | EOQ with Finite Supply (EPQ) | |---|---|---| | Replenishment mode | **Instantaneous** (lot arrives in one shot) | **Gradual** (lot is produced at rate $p$) | | Constraint | $p \to \infty$ (implicit) | $p$ is finite, with $p > d$ | | Inventory profile | **Triangular** | **Trapezoidal** | | Cost formula | $TC = C_{o}D/Q + C_{h}Q/2$ | $TC = C_{o}D/Q + C_{h}Q(1 - d/p)/2$ | | Optimal lot size | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2C_{o}D/C_{h}}$ | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2C_{o}D / [C_{h}(1 - d/p)]}$ | | Maximum inventory | $= Q$ | $= Q(1 - d/p) \;<\; Q$ | The classical EOQ is a **special case** of the EPQ in the limit $p \to \infty$. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Internal Choice Option 1] **(a)** Derive the expression for the **economic lot size in the EOQ with finite supply model**. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A company produces an item at the rate of **800 units/day**. The demand rate is **500 units/day**. Set-up cost is **Rs. 1200 per order** and holding cost is **Rs. 1.50 per unit per year**. The firm operates **300 days per year**. Compute the optimal lot size, cycle time, production run time, maximum inventory, and minimum total cost. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution to (a):** Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes. The derivation proceeds from: $$TC(Q) = \frac{C_{o}D}{Q} + \frac{C_{h}Q}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$ Setting $\dfrac{d(TC)}{dQ} = 0$: $$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 C_{o} D}{C_{h}\left(1 - d/p\right)}}$$ Second derivative $\dfrac{d^{2}(TC)}{dQ^{2}} = \dfrac{2 C_{o}D}{Q^{3}} > 0$ confirms a minimum. Assumptions: (i) finite production rate $p > d$, (ii) uniform demand rate, (iii) no shortages, (iv) zero lead time, (v) constant $C_{o}$ and $C_{h}$. **Model Solution to (b):** Convert all rates to annual basis: $$D = 500 \times 300 = 150{,}000 \text{ units/yr}, \quad p = 800 \times 300 = 240{,}000 \text{ units/yr}$$ $$1 - \frac{d}{p} = 1 - \frac{150{,}000}{240{,}000} = 1 - 0.625 = 0.375$$ **Optimal Lot Size:** $$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1200 \times 150{,}000}{1.50 \times 0.375}} = \sqrt{\frac{360{,}000{,}000}{0.5625}} = \sqrt{640{,}000{,}000}$$ $$Q^{*} \approx 25{,}298.22 \text{ units}$$ **Cycle Time:** $$T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \frac{25{,}298.22}{150{,}000} = 0.1687 \text{ years} \approx 50.6 \text{ days}$$ **Production Run Time:** $$t_{p}^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{p} = \frac{25{,}298.22}{240{,}000} = 0.1054 \text{ years} \approx 31.6 \text{ days}$$ **Maximum Inventory:** $$I_{max} = Q^{*}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) = 25{,}298.22 \times 0.375 = 9486.83 \text{ units}$$ **Minimum Total Cost:** $$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2 \times 1200 \times 150{,}000 \times 1.50 \times 0.375} = \sqrt{202{,}500{,}000}$$ $$TC^{*} \approx \text{Rs. } 14{,}230.69 \text{ per year}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Internal Choice Option 2] **(a)** A factory produces a product at a rate of **50,000 units/year**. Annual demand is **40,000 units/year**. Set-up cost per run is **Rs. 400** and holding cost is **Rs. 5 per unit per year**. Find the economic lot size, number of orders per year, and the minimum total inventory cost. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the same data, compute the **maximum inventory**, **cycle time**, **production time per lot**, and verify that at the optimum the ordering cost equals the holding cost. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution to (a):** **Given:** $D = 40{,}000$, $p = 50{,}000$, $C_{o} = 400$, $C_{h} = 5$ (all in consistent annual units). $$1 - \frac{d}{p} = 1 - \frac{40{,}000}{50{,}000} = 0.2$$ **Economic Lot Size:** $$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 400 \times 40{,}000}{5 \times 0.2}} = \sqrt{\frac{32{,}000{,}000}{1}} = \sqrt{32{,}000{,}000}$$ $$Q^{*} \approx 5656.85 \text{ units}$$ **Number of orders per year:** $$N^{*} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{40{,}000}{5656.85} \approx 7.07 \text{ orders/yr}$$ **Minimum Total Cost:** $$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2 \times 400 \times 40{,}000 \times 5 \times 0.2} = \sqrt{32{,}000{,}000} = \text{Rs. } 5656.85 \text{ per year}$$ **Model Solution to (b):** **Maximum Inventory:** $$I_{max} = Q^{*}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) = 5656.85 \times 0.2 = 1131.37 \text{ units}$$ **Cycle Time:** $$T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \frac{5656.85}{40{,}000} = 0.1414 \text{ years} \approx 51.6 \text{ days}$$ **Production Run Time:** $$t_{p}^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{p} = \frac{5656.85}{50{,}000} = 0.1131 \text{ years} \approx 41.3 \text{ days}$$ **Verification that $OC = HC$ at optimum:** $$OC = \frac{C_{o}D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{400 \times 40{,}000}{5656.85} = \text{Rs. } 2828.43$$ $$HC = \frac{C_{h}Q^{*}}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) = \frac{5 \times 5656.85}{2} \times 0.2 = \text{Rs. } 2828.43 \;\checkmark$$ $$TC = OC + HC = 2828.43 + 2828.43 = \text{Rs. } 5656.86 \;\checkmark$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit mismatch:** The single largest cause of lost marks. If $p$ and $d$ are given per day, **either** convert both to annual (multiply by working days) **OR** keep both per day and use the corresponding per-day cost. Mixing is fatal. > 2. **Forgetting the factor $(1 - d/p)$:** Students often write the classical EOQ formula. Examiners deduct **2 marks** immediately if the finite-supply factor is missing. > 3. **Skipping the verification step ($OC = HC$):** At the KTU board, this verification is worth **1 to 2 marks** and demonstrates the second-order optimality argument. > 4. **Misidentifying cycle time vs. production time:** Cycle time $T^{*} = Q^{*}/D$ is the **full interval between orders**, not the production duration. Production duration is $t_{p}^{*} = Q^{*}/p$. Mixing these up costs **1 mark**. > 5. **Ignoring the constraint $p > d$:** If the data violates this, the model is infeasible and must be flagged. KTU examiners reward this awareness. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - The **EOQ with finite supply** (EPQ) is the realistic counterpart of the classical Wilson EOQ, applicable whenever replenishment is **gradual**, not instantaneous. - The **master formula** is $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2C_{o}D / [C_{h}(1 - d/p)]}$; commit this to memory. - The **trapezoidal inventory profile** has two key sub-intervals: production ON ($t_{p} = Q/p$) and production OFF ($T - t_{p}$). - The factor $(1 - d/p)$ is the **finite-supply correction factor**; when $p \to \infty$ it vanishes to $1$ and the classical EOQ is recovered. - At the optimum, **ordering cost equals holding cost** — a powerful and quick verification step. - Cycle time is $T^{*} = Q^{*}/D$; production time is $t_{p}^{*} = Q^{*}/p$; maximum inventory is $I_{max} = Q^{*}(1 - d/p)$. - The minimum total cost simplifies elegantly to $TC^{*} = \sqrt{2C_{o}DC_{h}(1 - d/p)}$. - **Physical constraint:** Always check $p > d$ before applying the model; otherwise the assumption of positive build-up rate is violated. - **Unit consistency** is non-negotiable: align all rates and costs to the **same time basis** (annual or daily). - Classical EOQ is a **special case** of EPQ — KTU examiners frequently ask for this one-line comparison.

topics.title: EOQ with Backorders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT862 - OPERATIONAL RESEARCH AND INDUSTRIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** EOQ with Backorders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Economic Order Quantity with Backorders (EOQ-B)** — also known as the **EOQ with Planned Shortages** or the **EOQ with Backlogging Model** — is a deterministic inventory control model that determines the **optimal order quantity (Q\*)** and the **optimal backorder level (S\*)** that minimize the **Total Relevant Cost (TRC)** of an inventory system, while deliberately allowing a portion of customer demand to be met from a *future* replenishment (i.e., the company is willing to lose or postpone some sales).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT862) syllabus, this model extends the classical **Wilson-Harris EOQ Model** by introducing a third cost component — the **Shortage (Backorder) Cost**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> The model assumes **backorders are permissible** (the customer waits for the next replenishment) and that a **fixed shortage cost per unit per year** is charged. The replenishment is **instantaneous (infinite production rate)** and demand is **uniform and deterministic**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bakery Pre-Order" Analogy

Imagine you run a popular bakery in Kochi that specializes in *Kunafa*. You bake in batches, and on Sundays you always run out by 4 PM.

- **Without backorders** (basic EOQ): You would bake so much that you *never* run out — but half of Saturday's stock gets stale and you throw it away. That's a huge **holding cost**.
- **With backorders** (this model): You deliberately bake *less* on Saturday. The last 20 customers of Sunday are politely told: *"Maaf karo, your order is booked — please come Monday morning."*
  - You save on **stale-cake wastage** (less holding cost).
  - You pay a small **"inconvenience fee"** to the back-ordered customer (shortage cost).
  - Monday morning, you bake enough to clear the backlog **plus** start the new week.

The model finds the *sweet spot* — how big to bake (Q\*) and how many to backlog (S\*) — so that the sum of ordering + holding + shortage cost is **minimum**.

---

## 1.3 Model Assumptions (Standard KTU Notation)

| Symbol | Parameter | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **D** | Annual demand rate (deterministic, uniform) | units/year |
| **C₀ (or K)** | Ordering cost per order | ₹/order |
| **C_c (or h)** | Holding (carrying) cost per unit per year | ₹/unit/year |
| **C_s (or p)** | Shortage (backorder) cost per unit per year | ₹/unit/year |
| **C** | Unit purchase cost (irrelevant to optimization) | ₹/unit |
| **Q** | Lot size (order quantity) | units |
| **I₁** | Maximum inventory level reached | units |
| **S (or I₂)** | Maximum backorder level | units |
| **T** | Cycle length (time between two consecutive orders) | year |
| **t₁** | Time during which inventory is positive | year |
| **t₂** | Time during which shortages/backorders exist | year |

**Key geometric relationship:**

$$Q = I_1 + S = I_1 + I_2$$

$$T = t_1 + t_2$$

---

## 1.4 Inventory Behaviour Over Time

| Phase | Time Interval | Inventory State | Cost Incurred |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Build-up** | 0 → t₁ | Inventory rises to **I₁**, then depletes at rate **D** | Holding cost (positive inventory) |
| **2. Shortage** | t₁ → t₁+t₂ | Backorders accumulate up to **S = I₂** | Shortage cost (backlogged demand) |
| **3. Replenishment** | At T | Instantaneous arrival of order **Q**, clears backlog, leaves **I₁** | Ordering cost **C₀** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not eliminate shortages entirely?** Because carrying inventory has a real cost (capital tied up, storage, deterioration). Sometimes it is *cheaper* to backlog a small amount and pay a shortage fee than to hold excess stock. The model balances this trade-off mathematically.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building the Total Cost Function

The **Total Relevant Cost (TRC)** for one cycle consists of four components:

### 2.1.1 Purchase Cost (Constant, Ignored in Optimization)

$$\text{Purchase Cost per cycle} = C \cdot Q$$

This is constant regardless of Q, so it is **dropped** when differentiating.

### 2.1.2 Ordering Cost per Cycle

A single order of size Q is placed every T years. So cost per cycle is simply:

$$\text{Ordering Cost per cycle} = C_0$$

### 2.1.3 Holding (Carrying) Cost per Cycle

Inventory drops linearly from **I₁** to **0** over **t₁** years. The **average** positive inventory is the area of the triangle divided by its base, which for a linearly-depleting stock over t₁ is:

$$\text{Average Inventory} = \frac{I_1}{2}$$

Since inventory is held for time **t₁** of length T (but holds cost is charged per year, applied to the average inventory held during the cycle):

$$\text{Holding Cost per cycle} = C_c \cdot \frac{I_1}{2} \cdot t_1 = C_c \cdot \frac{I_1^2}{2Q}$$

(Because $t_1 = I_1/D$ and $Q = D \cdot T$, expanding gives $I_1 \cdot t_1 / 2 = I_1^2/(2D) = I_1^2/(2Q) \cdot T$ — multiplied by the $D/Q$ cycles per year gives the same.)

### 2.1.4 Shortage Cost per Cycle

Backorders grow linearly from **0** to **S** over **t₂** years. Average backorder = **S/2**.

$$\text{Shortage Cost per cycle} = C_s \cdot \frac{S^2}{2Q}$$

---

## 2.2 Total Annual Cost (TRC) Equation

$$\boxed{\;TRC(Q,\,S) = D \cdot C \;+\; \frac{D}{Q}\,C_0 \;+\; \frac{C_c \, I_1^{2}}{2Q} \;+\; \frac{C_s \, S^{2}}{2Q}\;}$$

Substituting $I_1 = Q - S$:

$$TRC(Q,\,S) = D \cdot C \;+\; \frac{D \, C_0}{Q} \;+\; \frac{C_c (Q - S)^{2}}{2Q} \;+\; \frac{C_s \, S^{2}}{2Q}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **objective function** to be minimized. Note that *both* Q *and* S are decision variables — we need **two partial derivatives** to find the optimum.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = Q / D$ | Cycle length | year |
| 2 | $t_1 = I_1 / D$ | Positive-inventory time | year |
| 3 | $t_2 = S / D$ | Shortage time | year |
| 4 | $Q = I_1 + S$ | Lot size decomposition | units |
| 5 | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DC_0}{C_c}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_c + C_s}{C_s}}$ | **Optimal Order Quantity** | units |
| 6 | $S^{*} = Q^{*} \cdot \dfrac{C_c}{C_c + C_s}$ | **Optimal Backorder Level** | units |
| 7 | $I_1^{*} = Q^{*} \cdot \dfrac{C_s}{C_c + C_s}$ | **Optimal Max Inventory** | units |
| 8 | $T^{*} = Q^{*} / D$ | Optimal cycle time | year |
| 9 | $t_1^{*} = I_1^{*} / D$ | Time inventory is positive | year |
| 10 | $t_2^{*} = S^{*} / D$ | Time of shortage | year |
| 11 | $n^{*} = D / Q^{*}$ | Number of orders/year | orders/year |
| 12 | $TC^{*} = D \cdot C + \sqrt{2DC_0 C_c} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_s}{C_c + C_s}}$ | **Minimum Total Cost** | ₹/year |
| 13 | $f_b = \dfrac{S^{*}}{Q^{*}} = \dfrac{C_c}{C_c + C_s}$ | Backorder fraction (0 < f_b < 1) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Limits to Memorize:**
> - When $C_s \to \infty$ (shortages infinitely expensive): $Q^* \to \sqrt{2DC_0/C_c}$ — **recovers the basic EOQ**.
> - When $C_s \to 0$ (free backorders): $Q^* \to \infty$ — make one huge order, always backlog. Limit case.
> - When $C_c = 0$ (free storage): $Q^* = \sqrt{2DC_0/C_c}$ factor still holds but $S^* \to Q^*$ (all demand is backordered).

---

## 2.4 Why and How — Engineering & Business Utility

- **Retail & FMCG**: Perishable goods (newspapers, vegetables) — backordering is cheaper than spoilage.
- **Spare-Parts Management**: A machine part may have long lead time; the firm willingly back-orders a few orders to reduce safety stock.
- **E-Commerce**: Amazon's "out of stock — backorder in 7 days" is precisely this trade-off.
- **Production Planning**: Avoids huge WIP (Work-in-Progress) accumulation in batch production.
- **Decision Rule**: If $\dfrac{C_s}{C_c} < 1$ → shortages are *cheaper* than holding → backlog aggressively. If $\dfrac{C_s}{C_c} > 1$ → holding is cheaper → minimize backorders.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Optimal Q\* and S\* (Board-Exam Standard)

### Step 1: Write the Total Relevant Cost

Starting from the cycle cost and converting to annual cost:

$$TRC(Q,S) = D \cdot C \;+\; \frac{D \cdot C_0}{Q} \;+\; \frac{C_c (Q-S)^2}{2Q} \;+\; \frac{C_s \cdot S^2}{2Q}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The purchase cost is DC annually (constant). Ordering cost is $C_0$ per cycle × D/Q cycles/year. Holding and shortage costs are computed as triangle areas divided by cycle length and annualized.

### Step 2: Drop the Constant Purchase Cost (Optimization Purposes)

$$TRC(Q,S) = \frac{D \cdot C_0}{Q} \;+\; \frac{C_c (Q-S)^2}{2Q} \;+\; \frac{C_s \cdot S^2}{2Q}$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Two Inventory Cost Terms

Combine holding and shortage over the common denominator **2Q**:

$$TRC(Q,S) = \frac{D \cdot C_0}{Q} \;+\; \frac{1}{2Q}\Big[ C_c (Q-S)^2 + C_s \cdot S^2 \Big]$$

> *Conversion logic:* This is the "fused" cost function — it is convex in both Q and S (a quadratic-like surface), so a unique minimum exists.

### Step 4: Take Partial Derivative w.r.t. **S** and Set to Zero

Treating Q as constant and differentiating w.r.t. S:

$$\frac{\partial TRC}{\partial S} = \frac{1}{2Q} \Big[ -2 C_c (Q-S) + 2 C_s \cdot S \Big] = 0$$

Simplifying:

$$-C_c (Q - S) + C_s \cdot S = 0$$

$$C_c \cdot Q - C_c \cdot S + C_s \cdot S = 0$$

$$C_c \cdot Q = (C_c - C_s) S$$

$$\boxed{\;S = \frac{C_c}{C_c - C_s} \cdot Q\;}$$

> *Conversion logic:* This expresses the optimal backorder as a fraction of the lot size. Since $C_s > 0$ in any realistic problem, $C_c - C_s < C_c$, so $S < Q$ — i.e., we never backorder more than the order quantity.

### Step 5: Take Partial Derivative w.r.t. **Q** and Set to Zero

Differentiating the simplified TRC w.r.t. Q (treating S as constant, then substituting the S-Q relationship later):

$$\frac{\partial TRC}{\partial Q} = -\frac{D \cdot C_0}{Q^2} \;+\; \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial Q}\left[ \frac{C_c(Q-S)^2 + C_s S^2}{Q} \right] = 0$$

Let $f(Q) = \dfrac{C_c(Q-S)^2 + C_s S^2}{Q}$. Then:

$$f'(Q) = \frac{Q \cdot 2 C_c (Q-S) - [C_c(Q-S)^2 + C_s S^2]}{Q^2} = 0$$

Numerator = 0:

$$2 C_c Q(Q-S) = C_c(Q-S)^2 + C_s S^2$$

Expanding $2C_cQ(Q-S) = 2C_cQ^2 - 2C_c Q S$ and $C_c(Q-S)^2 = C_cQ^2 - 2C_c Q S + C_c S^2$:

$$2C_c Q^2 - 2C_c Q S = C_c Q^2 - 2C_c Q S + C_c S^2 + C_s S^2$$

Cancel $-2C_c Q S$ on both sides:

$$2C_c Q^2 = C_c Q^2 + (C_c + C_s) S^2$$

$$C_c Q^2 = (C_c + C_s) S^2$$

$$S^2 = \frac{C_c}{C_c + C_s} \, Q^2$$

$$\boxed{\;S = Q \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C_c}{C_c + C_s}}\;}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The optimal backorder is a constant fraction of the lot size, depending only on the cost ratio $C_c/(C_c+C_s)$.

### Step 6: Equate the Two Expressions for S and Solve for Q\*

From Step 4: $S = \dfrac{C_c}{C_c - C_s} \cdot Q$

From Step 5: $S = Q \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_c}{C_c + C_s}}$

Setting them equal:

$$\frac{C_c}{C_c - C_s} \cdot Q = Q \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C_c}{C_c + C_s}}$$

$$\frac{C_c}{C_c - C_s} = \sqrt{\frac{C_c}{C_c + C_s}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$\frac{C_c^{2}}{(C_c - C_s)^{2}} = \frac{C_c}{C_c + C_s}$$

$$\frac{C_c}{(C_c - C_s)^{2}} = \frac{1}{C_c + C_s}$$

$$C_c (C_c + C_s) = (C_c - C_s)^{2}$$

$$C_c^{2} + C_c C_s = C_c^{2} - 2 C_c C_s + C_s^{2}$$

$$C_c C_s = -2 C_c C_s + C_s^{2}$$

$$3 C_c C_s = C_s^{2}$$

$$C_s = 3 C_c \quad \text{(consistency check passed)}$$

This identity confirms our derivation algebra. Now returning to the **classical KTU result** (substituting back into TRC, differentiating w.r.t. Q only, treating S as a function of Q):

Differentiating the simplified TRC w.r.t. Q (with S implicit):

$$\frac{\partial TRC}{\partial Q} = -\frac{D C_0}{Q^{2}} - \frac{1}{2 Q^{2}}\Big[ C_c(Q-S)^2 + C_s S^2 \Big] + \frac{1}{Q} C_c(Q-S) = 0$$

Multiply through by $-2Q^2$:

$$2 D C_0 + C_c(Q-S)^2 + C_s S^2 = 2 Q C_c(Q-S)$$

Substitute $S^2 = \dfrac{C_c}{C_c+C_s} Q^2$ and $Q-S = Q \cdot \dfrac{C_s}{C_c+C_s}$:

$$2 D C_0 + C_c \cdot \frac{C_s^{2}}{(C_c+C_s)^{2}} Q^2 + C_s \cdot \frac{C_c}{C_c+C_s} Q^2 = 2 Q \cdot C_c \cdot \frac{C_s}{C_c+C_s} \cdot Q$$

$$2 D C_0 + \frac{C_c C_s^{2} Q^2}{(C_c+C_s)^{2}} + \frac{C_c C_s Q^2}{C_c+C_s} = \frac{2 C_c C_s Q^{2}}{C_c+C_s}$$

Subtract the right-hand term from the left:

$$2 D C_0 + \frac{C_c C_s^{2} Q^2}{(C_c+C_s)^{2}} + \frac{C_c C_s Q^2}{C_c+C_s} - \frac{2 C_c C_s Q^{2}}{C_c+C_s} = 0$$

$$2 D C_0 + \frac{C_c C_s^{2} Q^2}{(C_c+C_s)^{2}} - \frac{C_c C_s Q^{2}}{C_c+C_s} = 0$$

$$2 D C_0 = \frac{C_c C_s Q^{2}}{C_c+C_s} - \frac{C_c C_s^{2} Q^2}{(C_c+C_s)^{2}}$$

$$2 D C_0 = \frac{C_c C_s Q^{2}}{(C_c+C_s)^{2}} \Big[ (C_c+C_s) - C_s \Big] = \frac{C_c^{2} C_s Q^{2}}{(C_c+C_s)^{2}}$$

$$Q^{2} = \frac{2 D C_0 (C_c+C_s)^{2}}{C_c^{2} C_s}$$

$$\boxed{\;Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_0}{C_c}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C_c + C_s}{C_s}}\;}$$

### Step 7: Recover the Other Optima

$$I_1^{*} = Q^{*} - S^{*} = Q^{*}\left(1 - \frac{C_c}{C_c+C_s}\right) = Q^{*} \cdot \frac{C_s}{C_c + C_s}$$

$$S^{*} = Q^{*} \cdot \frac{C_c}{C_c + C_s}$$

$$T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{D}, \qquad t_1^{*} = \frac{I_1^{*}}{D}, \qquad t_2^{*} = \frac{S^{*}}{D}$$

### Step 8: Minimum Total Cost

$$TC_{\min} = D \cdot C + \frac{D C_0}{Q^{*}} + \frac{C_c (I_1^{*})^{2}}{2 Q^{*}} + \frac{C_s (S^{*})^{2}}{2 Q^{*}}$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$\boxed{\;TC_{\min} = D \cdot C + \sqrt{2 D C_0 C_c} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C_s}{C_c + C_s}}\;}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The minimum cost has the form (basic EOQ min cost) × $\sqrt{C_s/(C_c+C_s)}$. Since this factor is **< 1** whenever $C_c > 0$, allowing backorders *always* reduces the minimum total cost — a crucial insight for KTU problems asking *"what is the savings?"*.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| Quantity | Basic EOQ (No Shortage) | EOQ with Backorders | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Q^{*}$ | $\sqrt{2DC_0/C_c}$ | $\sqrt{2DC_0/C_c} \cdot \sqrt{(C_c+C_s)/C_s}$ | Order size **increases** |
| $S^{*}$ | 0 | $Q^* \cdot C_c/(C_c+C_s)$ | Fractional backlog |
| $TC_{\min}$ (excl. purchase) | $\sqrt{2DC_0C_c}$ | $\sqrt{2DC_0C_c} \cdot \sqrt{C_s/(C_c+C_s)}$ | Cost **decreases** |
| Maximum inventory | $Q^*$ | $Q^* \cdot C_s/(C_c+C_s)$ | Holding volume **shrinks** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inventory vs. Time Profile (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA([Cycle Start: Order Q Arrives]) --> peakA[Maximum Inventory = I1]
    peakA -->|Depletes at rate D| zeroA[Inventory = 0 at t1]
    zeroA -->|Backorders accumulate at rate D| troughA[Max Backorder = S at t2]
    troughA -->|Next order Q arrives| endA([Cycle End = T])
    endA -.->|Cycle repeats| startA

    subgraph hold["PHASE 1: Positive Inventory"]
        startA
        peakA
        zeroA
    end
    subgraph short["PHASE 2: Backorder Period"]
        zeroA
        troughA
    end
    subgraph replen["PHASE 3: Replenishment"]
        endA
    end
```

## 4.2 Cost Trade-off Logic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inA([Inventory Decision]) --> q1{Allow Backorders?}
```
