```mermaid
    q1 -- No --> branchBasic[Basic EOQ Model]
    q1 -- Yes --> branchBack[EOQ with Backorders]
    branchBasic --> formulaBasic["Q* = sqrt(2 D C0 / Cc)"]
    branchBack --> formulaBack["Q* = sqrt(2 D C0 / Cc) * sqrt((Cc + Cs) / Cs)"]
    formulaBack --> comp1[Higher Order Q]
    formulaBack --> comp2[Lower Holding Cost]
    formulaBack --> comp3[New Shortage Cost Cs]
    comp1 --> outA([Optimum: Minimum TRC])
    comp2 --> outA
    comp3 --> outA
    branchBasic --> outB([Cost = sqrt(2 D C0 Cc)])
    outA --> compare{Compare Both Costs}
    outB --> compare
    compare --> result["TRC_backorder is ALWAYS less than TRC_basic"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — When to Backorder?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Given D, C0, Cc, Cs]) --> computeQ[Compute Q* and S*]
    computeQ --> checkS{S* greater than 0?}
    checkS -- Yes --> viable[Backorder Model Viable]
    checkS -- No --> notViable[Falls back to Basic EOQ]
    viable --> checkMag{S* / Q* greater than 0.5?}
    checkMag -- Yes --> heavy[Aggressive Backordering]
    checkMag -- No --> light[Conservative Backordering]
    heavy --> note1["Cc much greater than Cs: storage is expensive"]
    light --> note2["Cs much greater than Cc: shortage is expensive"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMUT862]**
**Define "Backorder" in inventory management. Under what condition does a firm prefer backordering over holding inventory?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **backorder** is an unfilled customer demand that the firm accepts as a commitment to deliver from a *future* replenishment. The customer waits; the sale is not lost.

A firm prefers backordering over holding inventory when the **shortage cost (C_s) is less than the holding cost (C_c)** — i.e., it is cheaper to *promise and delay* than to *store and carry*. Mathematically, the decision rule is $C_s < C_c$ (or, more precisely, when the optimal $S^* > 0$ under the EOQ-B model).

*(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: condition; 1 mark: link to cost trade-off.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional Exam PEMUT862]**
**State the optimal order quantity (Q\*) and backorder level (S\*) for the EOQ model with backorders. Mention one advantage and one limitation of this model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_0}{C_c}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C_c + C_s}{C_s}}$$

$$S^{*} = Q^{*} \cdot \frac{C_c}{C_c + C_s}$$

**Advantage:** Reduces total inventory cost compared to the basic EOQ (by a factor of $\sqrt{C_s/(C_c+C_s)}$).

**Limitation:** Assumes customers are willing to wait without switching to competitors (no lost sales).

*(1 mark each for Q\* and S\*, 1 mark for advantage/limitation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**

A manufacturing firm uses **12,000 units/year** of a raw material. The ordering cost is **₹150/order**, the holding cost is **₹6/unit/year**, and the shortage (backorder) cost is **₹4/unit/year**. The firm is willing to backorder.

**Find:**
**(a)** The optimal order quantity (Q\*) and the optimal backorder level (S\*). (7 marks)
**(b)** The minimum total inventory cost per year, the cycle time, and the time of shortage. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**Given:** $D = 12{,}000$ units/year, $C_0 = ₹150$/order, $C_c = ₹6$/unit/year, $C_s = ₹4$/unit/year.

##### Part (a) — Compute Q\* and S\*

Using the formulas:

$$\frac{C_c + C_s}{C_s} = \frac{6 + 4}{4} = \frac{10}{4} = 2.5$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{2 D C_0}{C_c}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12000 \times 150}{6}} = \sqrt{\frac{3{,}600{,}000}{6}} = \sqrt{600{,}000}$$

$$\sqrt{600{,}000} = \sqrt{6 \times 10^{5}} = \sqrt{6} \times \sqrt{10^{5}} \approx 2.449 \times 316.228 \approx 774.6$$

$$Q^{*} = 774.6 \times \sqrt{2.5} = 774.6 \times 1.5811 \approx 1224.7 \text{ units}$$

$$S^{*} = Q^{*} \cdot \frac{C_c}{C_c + C_s} = 1224.7 \times \frac{6}{10} = 1224.7 \times 0.6 = 734.8 \text{ units}$$

$$I_1^{*} = Q^{*} - S^{*} = 1224.7 - 734.8 = 489.9 \text{ units}$$

**Answers for (a):**
- $Q^* \approx 1225$ units (7 marks allocation: 3 for Q\*, 2 for S\*, 2 for I₁\*)

---

##### Part (b) — Compute Minimum Total Cost, Cycle Time, Shortage Time

**Cycle Time:**

$$T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \frac{1224.7}{12000} \approx 0.1021 \text{ year} \approx 37.3 \text{ days}$$

**Time of Shortage:**

$$t_2^{*} = \frac{S^{*}}{D} = \frac{734.8}{12000} \approx 0.0612 \text{ year} \approx 22.4 \text{ days}$$

**Time of Positive Inventory:**

$$t_1^{*} = \frac{I_1^{*}}{D} = \frac{489.9}{12000} \approx 0.0408 \text{ year} \approx 14.9 \text{ days}$$

(Check: $t_1 + t_2 = 14.9 + 22.4 = 37.3$ days = T\* ✓)

**Minimum Total Cost (excluding purchase cost):**

$$TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 D C_0 C_c} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C_s}{C_c + C_s}}$$

$$\sqrt{2 \times 12000 \times 150 \times 6} = \sqrt{21{,}600{,}000} \approx 4647.6$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{C_s}{C_c + C_s}} = \sqrt{\frac{4}{10}} = \sqrt{0.4} \approx 0.6325$$

$$TC_{\min} \approx 4647.6 \times 0.6325 \approx 2939.5 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Answers for (b):**
- $T^* \approx 0.1021$ year (≈ 37.3 days)
- $t_2^* \approx 0.0612$ year (≈ 22.4 days)
- $TC_{\min} \approx ₹2940$/year
(7 marks: 2 for T\*, 2 for t₂\*, 3 for TC_min including correct formula application)

**Verification via direct substitution:**

$$\text{Ordering} = \frac{D \cdot C_0}{Q^*} = \frac{12000 \times 150}{1224.7} \approx 1469.7 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\text{Holding} = \frac{C_c (I_1^*)^2}{2 Q^*} = \frac{6 \times (489.9)^2}{2 \times 1224.7} = \frac{6 \times 240{,}002}{2449.4} \approx 587.9 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\text{Shortage} = \frac{C_s (S^*)^2}{2 Q^*} = \frac{4 \times (734.8)^2}{2 \times 1224.7} = \frac{4 \times 539{,}931}{2449.4} \approx 881.7 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\text{Total} = 1469.7 + 587.9 + 881.7 = 2939.3 \text{ ₹} \approx TC_{\min} \;\; ✓$$

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]**

A company sells **5000 units/year** of a product. The cost of placing an order is **₹80**, the holding cost is **₹10/unit/year**, and the shortage cost is **₹15/unit/year**. The company allows backordering.

**Determine:**
**(a)** The EOQ, the backorder quantity, and the maximum inventory. (7 marks)
**(b)** The number of orders per year, total time of shortage per year, and the total minimum cost. Also state the percentage savings over the basic EOQ model. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**Given:** $D = 5000$ units/year, $C_0 = ₹80$/order, $C_c = ₹10$/unit/year, $C_s = ₹15$/unit/year.

##### Part (a) — EOQ, S\*, I₁\*

$$\sqrt{\frac{2 D C_0}{C_c}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times 80}{10}} = \sqrt{80{,}000} \approx 282.84$$

$$\frac{C_c + C_s}{C_s} = \frac{10 + 15}{15} = \frac{25}{15} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.6667$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}} = \sqrt{1.6667} \approx 1.2910$$

$$Q^{*} = 282.84 \times 1.2910 \approx 365.15 \approx 365 \text{ units}$$

$$S^{*} = Q^{*} \cdot \frac{C_c}{C_c + C_s} = 365.15 \times \frac{10}{25} = 365.15 \times 0.4 = 146.06 \text{ units}$$

$$I_1^{*} = Q^{*} - S^{*} = 365.15 - 146.06 = 219.09 \text{ units}$$

(7 marks: 3 for Q\*, 2 for S\*, 2 for I₁\*)

---

##### Part (b) — Number of Orders, Shortage Time, Minimum Cost, Savings

**Number of orders per year:**

$$n^{*} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{5000}{365.15} \approx 13.69 \text{ orders/year}$$

**Shortage time per cycle:**

$$t_2^{*} = \frac{S^{*}}{D} = \frac{146.06}{5000} = 0.02921 \text{ year} \approx 10.66 \text{ days}$$

**Total shortage time per year:**

$$= n^{*} \times t_2^{*} = 13.69 \times 0.02921 \approx 0.4 \text{ year}$$

(Or simply: $S^* \times D/Q^* = 146.06 / 365.15 = 0.4$ year ≈ 146 days)

**Minimum Total Cost (excluding purchase cost):**

$$TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 \times 5000 \times 80 \times 10} \times \sqrt{\frac{15}{25}}$$

$$\sqrt{8{,}000{,}000} = 2828.43, \qquad \sqrt{0.6} = 0.7746$$

$$TC_{\min} = 2828.43 \times 0.7746 \approx 2190.7 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Basic EOQ Total Cost (for comparison):**

$$TC_{\text{basic}} = \sqrt{2 D C_0 C_c} = \sqrt{8{,}000{,}000} = 2828.43 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Percentage Savings:**

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{TC_{\text{basic}} - TC_{\min}}{TC_{\text{basic}}} \times 100\% = \left(1 - \sqrt{\frac{C_s}{C_c + C_s}}\right) \times 100\%$$

$$= (1 - 0.7746) \times 100\% = 22.54\%$$

**Verification (per component):**

- Ordering: $D C_0/Q^* = 5000 \times 80 / 365.15 = 1095.3$ ₹
- Holding: $C_c (I_1^*)^2 / (2 Q^*) = 10 \times 219.09^2 / (2 \times 365.15) = 657.0$ ₹
- Shortage: $C_s (S^*)^2 / (2 Q^*) = 15 \times 146.06^2 / (2 \times 365.15) = 438.2$ ₹
- **Total = 1095.3 + 657.0 + 438.2 = 2190.5 ₹** ✓

(7 marks: 2 for n\*, 2 for shortage time, 3 for minimum cost & savings percentage)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor** $\sqrt{(C_c+C_s)/C_s}$ — students often plug into basic EOQ formula and lose 4–5 marks immediately.
> 2. **Mixing up S\* and I₁\*** — remember: $S^*$ (backorder) is the *larger* one when $C_c < C_s$, but the *smaller* one when $C_c > C_s$ (as in Q1). The geometric relation $Q^* = I_1^* + S^*$ **must** be checked in every problem.
> 3. **Forgetting to compute $t_1^*$ and $t_2^*$** when the question asks for "time of shortage" — direct division by D is required.
> 4. **Unit errors** — if demand is given *per month*, convert to *per year* **before** applying formulas. The KTU paper often uses monthly rates to test this.
> 5. **Ignoring purchase cost DC** — never add it inside the square root. It is a constant and is added (not optimized) to the final answer.
> 6. **Rounding too early** — keep at least 4 significant digits until the final step; otherwise the verification step will show a mismatch of several rupees, costing you 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 High-Density Revision Checklist for EOQ with Backorders**

- **Two decision variables**: Q (lot size) **and** S (backorder level). Both must be optimized.
- **Core cost equation**: $TRC = D \cdot C + \dfrac{D C_0}{Q} + \dfrac{C_c (Q-S)^2}{2Q} + \dfrac{C_s S^2}{2Q}$
- **Optimal Q\*** has **two multiplicative factors**: basic EOQ × backorder factor $\sqrt{(C_c+C_s)/C_s}$
- **Optimal S\*** is always a **fraction** of Q\*: $S^* = Q^* \cdot C_c / (C_c + C_s)$
- **Maximum inventory** I₁\* = $Q^* \cdot C_s / (C_c + C_s)$
- **Cycle time decomposition**: $T = t_1 + t_2 = I_1/D + S/D = Q/D$
- **Minimum cost formula** (excl. purchase): $\sqrt{2 D C_0 C_c} \cdot \sqrt{C_s/(C_c+C_s)}$
- **Limiting case 1**: $C_s \to \infty$ ⇒ model collapses to **basic EOQ** (no backorders).
- **Limiting case 2**: $C_c = 0$ ⇒ infinite Q\*, all demand backordered (impractical).
- **Decision rule**: If $C_s > C_c$ → small backorders; if $C_s < C_c$ → large backorders.
- **Always true**: $TC_{\text{backorder}} \le TC_{\text{basic EOQ}}$ — backorders *always* reduce cost.
- **Geometric check**: Plot inventory vs. time — should be a triangle dipping **below** the axis (negative) for the backorder phase.
- **Assumption to state explicitly in answers**: instantaneous replenishment, deterministic demand, backorders fully backlogged (not lost).
- **Common KTU numerical "twist"**: demand is given monthly — **convert to annual** by multiplying by 12.
- **Verification habit**: After computing Q\*, plug back into TRC to confirm — catches arithmetic errors.
- **Mention units** in every numerical step (units/year, ₹/order, etc.) — KTU markers reward this.
- **Common alternative form**: $Q^* = \sqrt{2 K D / h} \cdot \sqrt{(h+p)/p}$ — same formula, different notation.
- **Comparison with EOQ-with-shortage-lost-sales model**: here, S = Q (full backorder), but lost-sales version has S as a *fraction of demand lost* — different objective function.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EOQ with Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT862 - OPERATIONAL RESEARCH AND INDUSTRIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** EOQ with Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EOQ with Constraints** is a modified version of the classical Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) model in which the basic assumption of *unlimited availability* of resources (storage space, working capital, number of orders, etc.) is relaxed. The optimum order quantity is then obtained by **minimizing the Total Variable Cost subject to one or more practical limitations**, typically handled using the **Lagrange Multiplier Method** or by direct substitution at the constraint boundary.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEMUT862, Module 4), the two most frequently tested constrained EOQ variants are:

1. **EOQ with Storage Space (Warehouse) Constraint** — the most commonly examined.
2. **EOQ with Capital (Investment) Constraint**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a small kirana shop owner who buys rice in bulk. Buying **too much** saves trips (low ordering cost) but he runs out of shelf space — his son slips, bags break, and he incurs *damage/holding cost*. Buying **too little** keeps shelves airy but he visits the wholesaler every other day (high ordering cost & transport cost). The classical EOQ gives the mathematically perfect balance, **but only if his godown is infinitely large and his cash is unlimited**.

In real life, his godown is only **1,500 sq ft** and his working capital is only **₹2,00,000**. So the "perfect" EOQ is now *infeasible*. The owner must either:

- Order the **maximum quantity that physically fits** in his godown (boundary substitution), or
- Find a **new optimum** that balances ordering cost, holding cost, *and* the opportunity cost of misusing the scarce resource (Lagrange multiplier).

That "scarce resource penalty" is precisely what the **Lagrange multiplier (λ)** represents.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **D** — Annual demand in units
- **O** — Ordering cost per order in **₹/order**
- **C** — Carrying (holding) cost per unit per year in **₹/unit/year**
- **P** — Purchase price per unit in **₹/unit**
- **q** — Floor space occupied by one unit in **sq ft/unit**
- **W** — Maximum available warehouse floor space in **sq ft**
- **λ** — Lagrange multiplier (shadow price of the scarce resource)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A 14-mark question from this module almost always asks you to (i) verify whether the classical EOQ violates the constraint, and (ii) if it does, recompute the *new* optimum using the Lagrange method. Skipping step (i) is the #1 reason students lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breaking Down the Logic

The classical EOQ is derived under the **assumption of no constraints**:

$$Q_{classical} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot D \cdot O}{C}}$$

When constraints exist, we must **minimize a cost function under a side condition**, which is a classic **constrained optimization** problem.

### Step-by-Step Logical Flow

1. **Write the total variable cost (TVC) function:**

$$\text{TVC} = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q}\cdot O}_{\text{Ordering Cost}} + \underbrace{D \cdot P}_{\text{Purchase Cost (constant)}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2}\cdot C}_{\text{Carrying Cost}}$$

2. **Identify the binding constraint:** For a warehouse-space problem it is

$$q \cdot Q \leq W$$

3. **Form the Lagrangian function** by adding the constraint multiplied by the Lagrange multiplier λ:

$$L(Q, \lambda) = \frac{D \cdot O}{Q} + D \cdot P + \frac{Q \cdot C}{2} + \lambda \cdot (W - q \cdot Q)$$

4. **Apply the first-order necessary conditions (FONC):**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial Q} = -\frac{D \cdot O}{Q^{2}} + \frac{C}{2} - \lambda \cdot q = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \lambda} = W - q \cdot Q = 0$$

5. **Solve simultaneously:** The second equation forces the constraint to be *binding* (just fully utilized), and the first gives the **shadow price** of warehouse space.

6. **Rearrange to obtain the constrained EOQ formula:**

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot D \cdot O}{C + 2 \cdot \lambda \cdot q}}$$

7. **Iterative solution procedure:** Because λ is unknown, we test values of λ until the constraint is exactly satisfied (`q·Q* = W`).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_{0} = \sqrt{2DO/C}$ | Classical (unconstrained) EOQ | units |
| $q \cdot Q \leq W$ | Storage space constraint | sq ft |
| $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DO/(C + 2\lambda q)}$ | Constrained EOQ with Lagrange multiplier | units |
| $\text{TVC} = (D/Q)O + (Q/2)C$ | Total variable cost (ignoring constant D·P) | ₹/year |
| $\lambda = (C/2 - DO/Q^{2})/q$ | Optimal Lagrange multiplier (shadow price) | ₹/sq ft |
| $Q \cdot P/2 \leq A$ | Capital constraint (A = available capital) | ₹ |
| $N^{*} = D/Q^{*}$ | Optimal number of orders per year | orders/year |
| $T^{*} = Q^{*}/D$ | Optimal cycle time | years |
| If $q \cdot Q_{0} > W$ | Constraint is *violated* — use Lagrange | — |
| If $q \cdot Q_{0} \leq W$ | Constraint is *non-binding* — classical EOQ is optimal | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind the math:** The term $2 \lambda q$ is *added* to the denominator of the EOQ formula. A higher λ means space is more "expensive" to use, so the model automatically orders **smaller batches** to free up room — exactly the intuitive behaviour we expect.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

EOQ with constraints is used in:

- **Retail & FMCG chains** — shelf space allocation in supermarkets.
- **Automotive assembly lines** — limited floor space in the parts warehouse near the line.
- **Pharmaceutical inventory** — cold-storage rooms have hard capacity limits.
- **E-commerce fulfilment centres** — capital is locked in safety stock; firms must balance investment vs. ordering frequency.
- **Defence logistics** — limited bunker capacity for ammunition/ration stock.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Storage Space Constraint

### Step 1 — Formulate the objective function

The total cost to be minimised is the sum of ordering + holding + purchasing cost. The purchasing cost $D \cdot P$ is constant w.r.t. Q, so it can be dropped for minimisation purposes:

$$f(Q) = \frac{D \cdot O}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot C}{2}$$

### Step 2 — State the constraint

$$g(Q) = q \cdot Q - W \leq 0$$

### Step 3 — Form the Lagrangian

$$L(Q, \lambda) = \frac{D \cdot O}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot C}{2} + \lambda \cdot (W - q \cdot Q)$$

where $\lambda \geq 0$ is the Lagrange multiplier.

### Step 4 — First-order necessary conditions

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial Q} = -\frac{D \cdot O}{Q^{2}} + \frac{C}{2} - \lambda \cdot q = 0 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \lambda} = W - q \cdot Q = 0 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

### Step 5 — Solve equation (i) for Q

Rearranging (i):

$$\lambda \cdot q = \frac{C}{2} - \frac{D \cdot O}{Q^{2}}$$

$$\frac{C}{2} + \lambda \cdot q = \frac{D \cdot O}{Q^{2}}$$

$$Q^{2} = \frac{2 \cdot D \cdot O}{C + 2 \cdot \lambda \cdot q}$$

Taking the positive root (Q > 0):

$$Q^{*}(\lambda) = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot D \cdot O}{C + 2 \cdot \lambda \cdot q}}$$

### Step 6 — Enforce the constraint (ii)

From (ii):

$$Q_{max} = \frac{W}{q}$$

The optimal Q must satisfy $Q^{*}(\lambda) = W/q$. Since λ is unknown explicitly, we use the **trial-and-error / iterative procedure**:

| Trial | λ | Q* = √(2DO/(C+2λq)) | q·Q* | Constraint Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 (classical) | $\sqrt{2DO/C}$ | $> W$? | If yes → start increasing λ |
| 2 | $\lambda_{1}$ | recompute | check | Move λ up if $qQ^{*} > W$, down if $qQ^{*} < W$ |
| 3 | $\lambda_{2}$ | recompute | check | Refine |
| ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | Stop when $q \cdot Q^{*} = W$ |

### Step 7 — Compute total cost at Q*

$$\text{TVC}^{*} = \frac{D \cdot O}{Q^{*}} + \frac{Q^{*} \cdot C}{2} + D \cdot P$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KKT Complementary Slackness condition:** $\lambda \cdot (W - qQ) = 0$. Since at optimum $qQ = W$, the constraint is *active*, which means λ > 0. The shadow price λ literally tells you **how much total cost would fall (per unit of additional space)** if the warehouse were expanded by 1 sq ft — a hugely powerful managerial insight.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Full Solution

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A retail chain has annual demand **D = 10,000 units**, ordering cost **O = ₹200/order**, carrying cost **C = ₹10/unit/year**, floor space per unit **q = 4 sq ft/unit**, and total available warehouse space **W = 1,500 sq ft**. Purchase price P = ₹50/unit. Find the optimal order quantity using the Lagrange method.

### Step 1 — Compute classical EOQ

$$Q_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 10000 \cdot 200}{10}} = \sqrt{400000} \approx 632.46 \text{ units}$$

### Step 2 — Check constraint

$$q \cdot Q_{0} = 4 \times 632.46 = 2529.82 \text{ sq ft} \; > \; 1500 \text{ sq ft}$$

Constraint is **violated** → classical EOQ is *infeasible*. Lagrange method required.

### Step 3 — Iterative trials for λ

| Trial | λ | $C + 2\lambda q$ | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{4{,}000{,}000/(C+2\lambda q)}$ | $q \cdot Q^{*}$ (sq ft) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.0 | 10.0 | 632.46 | 2529.82 | Way over |
| 2 | 2.0 | 26.0 | 392.23 | 1568.92 | Slightly over |
| 3 | 2.4 | 29.2 | 370.12 | 1480.50 | Under |
| 4 | 2.35 | 28.8 | 372.68 | 1490.70 | Under (very close) |
| 5 | 2.32 | 28.56 | 374.17 | 1496.70 | Under (≈ at boundary) |
| 6 | 2.30 | 28.4 | 375.42 | 1501.70 | Just over |

Optimal at **λ ≈ 2.32**, **Q* ≈ 374 units**.

### Step 4 — Total cost at optimum

$$\text{Ordering Cost} = \frac{10000}{374} \times 200 = 5347.59 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\text{Carrying Cost} = \frac{374}{2} \times 10 = 1870.00 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\text{Purchase Cost} = 10000 \times 50 = 500000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\boxed{\text{TVC}^{*} = 5347.59 + 1870.00 + 500000 = 507217.59 \text{ ₹/year}}$$

### Step 5 — Managerial insight (shadow price)

Each additional sq ft of warehouse space would reduce TVC by approximately **λ ≈ ₹2.32/sq ft/year**. Hence, if the firm can rent extra space at less than ₹2.32/sq ft/year, it should!

## 3.3 Derivation: Capital (Working-Capital) Constraint

### Formulation

Average inventory value = $(Q/2) \cdot P$. The capital constraint states:

$$\frac{Q \cdot P}{2} \leq A \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q \leq \frac{2A}{P}$$

### Lagrangian

$$L(Q,\lambda) = \frac{D \cdot O}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot C}{2} + \lambda \cdot \left(\frac{2A}{P} - Q\right)$$

### FONC

$$-\frac{D \cdot O}{Q^{2}} + \frac{C}{2} - \lambda = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot D \cdot O}{C + 2\lambda}}$$

Iterate λ until $Q = 2A/P$.

### Boundary Substitution Shortcut

If classical $Q_0 \leq 2A/P$, no constraint binding → use $Q_0$.

If classical $Q_0 > 2A/P$, capital binding → simply take $Q^{*} = 2A/P$ (use the entire available capital), provided carrying cost penalty of forcing Q up is less than ordering-cost savings. Otherwise, use Lagrange.

> [!TIP]
> **Comparison rule of thumb:** If the constraint is a *hard cap* (cannot be exceeded at all), use **boundary substitution** $Q^* = 2A/P$. If the constraint is a *soft cost penalty*, use the **Lagrange multiplier** method.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart — Constrained EOQ Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: Given D, O, C, q, W]) --> B[Compute Classical EOQ: Q0 = sqrt(2DO/C)]
    B --> C{Check q * Q0 ≤ W?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Constraint Non-Binding]
    D --> E[Optimal Q* = Q0]
    C -- No --> F[Constraint Violated]
    F --> G[Form Lagrangian L = DO/Q + QC/2 + lambda times W - qQ]
    G --> H[Set Initial lambda = 0]
    H --> I[Compute Q lambda = sqrt 2DO divided by C + 2 lambda q]
    I --> J{Check q * Q lambda ≈ W?}
    J -- Not Equal --> K[Adjust lambda: Increase if qQ > W; Decrease if qQ < W]
    K --> I
    J -- Approximately Equal --> L[Optimal Q* and lambda found]
    L --> M[Compute TVC at Q*]
    E --> M
    M --> N([Output: Q*, lambda, TVC, N*, T*])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Constrained Inventory System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        A1[Annual Demand D] --> F1
        A2[Ordering Cost O] --> F1
        A3[Holding Cost C] --> F1
        A4[Storage per unit q] --> F1
        A5[Warehouse W] --> F1
    end
    subgraph ENGINE[Optimization Engine]
        F1[Classical EOQ Calculator] --> F2[Constraint Validator]
        F2 --> F3[Lagrange Solver Module]
        F3 --> F4[Iterative lambda Adjuster]
        F4 --> F3
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        F3 --> O1[Optimal Order Q*]
        F3 --> O2[Shadow Price lambda]
        F3 --> O3[Number of Orders N*]
        F3 --> O4[Total Cost TVC*]
        F3 --> O5[Cycle Time T*]
    end
```

## 4.3 Cost-vs-Quantity Trade-off Visualisation (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOWQ[Small Q Region]
        A1[Ordering Cost: HIGH] --> X1
        A2[Carrying Cost: LOW] --> X1
        A3[Space Used: OK] --> X1
    end
    subgraph MIDQ[Balanced Q Region]
        B1[Ordering Cost: MODERATE] --> X2
        B2[Carrying Cost: MODERATE] --> X2
        B3[Space Used: APPROACHING LIMIT] --> X2
    end
    subgraph HIGHQ[Large Q Region - FORBIDDEN by constraint]
        C1[Ordering Cost: LOW] --> X3
        C2[Carrying Cost: HIGH] --> X3
        C3[Space Used: EXCEEDS W] --> X3
        X3 -.->|Infeasible Zone| D[Constraint Boundary]
    end
    D --> X2
    D --> X1
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What do you mean by EOQ with constraints? Why is the classical EOQ formula insufficient in real inventory systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
EOQ with constraints refers to the determination of the most economical order quantity when practical limitations such as available **storage space, working capital, or maximum number of orders** are imposed on the system (1 mark). The classical EOQ formula $Q = \sqrt{2DO/C}$ assumes **unlimited storage, unlimited capital, and no restriction on order frequency** (1 mark). In real industries, warehouses have finite floor space and firms have a limited capital budget, so the unconstrained optimum may be **infeasible**, requiring a constrained optimisation using the **Lagrange Multiplier method** (1 mark).

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the KKT complementary slackness condition for the storage-constrained EOQ problem and explain its managerial meaning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The KKT complementary slackness condition is $\lambda \cdot (W - qQ) = 0$ with $\lambda \geq 0$ and $W - qQ \geq 0$ (1 mark). This means either the constraint is *inactive* ($\lambda = 0$ and $W > qQ$) **or** the constraint is *active* ($\lambda > 0$ and $W = qQ$) (1 mark). Managerially, the value of λ (called the **shadow price**) tells the decision-maker **how much the total cost would decrease per unit increase in warehouse space** — guiding make-vs-rent decisions for storage expansion (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Failing to **first check the constraint** with classical EOQ costs 2 marks.
> - Not stating units in the final answer costs 1 mark.
> - Skipping the **shadow-price managerial interpretation** loses 2 marks.
> - Forgetting that $D \cdot P$ is constant (and can be dropped) leads to unnecessary, error-prone calculations — and usually 1 mark deduction.
> - Showing only one trial value of λ without iteration is treated as incomplete (–2 marks).

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — Storage-Space Constrained EOQ

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**
>
> A manufacturing company consumes **20,000 units** of a raw material per year. The ordering cost is **₹150 per order**, the carrying cost is **₹12 per unit per year**, and the unit purchase price is **₹40**. Each unit occupies **2 sq ft** of floor space, and the total warehouse area available is **2,000 sq ft**.
>
> **(a)** Verify whether the classical EOQ satisfies the storage-space constraint. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** If the constraint is violated, determine the **optimal order quantity, optimal Lagrange multiplier, optimal number of orders, and minimum total cost** using the Lagrange multiplier method. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Given:** D = 20,000 units/yr, O = ₹150/order, C = ₹12/unit/yr, P = ₹40/unit, q = 2 sq ft/unit, W = 2,000 sq ft.

### Part (a) — Verification of Constraint

**Step 1.** Compute the classical EOQ.

$$Q_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 20000 \cdot 150}{12}} = \sqrt{\frac{6{,}000{,}000}{12}} = \sqrt{500000} \approx 707.11 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2.** Compute the floor space required by $Q_0$.

$$q \cdot Q_{0} = 2 \times 707.11 = 1414.21 \text{ sq ft}$$

**Step 3.** Compare with available space W.

$$q \cdot Q_{0} = 1414.21 \text{ sq ft} \; \leq \; 2000 \text{ sq ft} = W$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The classical EOQ **satisfies** the storage-space constraint because 1414.21 sq ft < 2000 sq ft. Therefore, the **unconstrained EOQ is itself optimal**, and the Lagrange method is *not required*. The constraint is **non-binding** (KKT case: λ = 0).

**Step 4.** Report optimal parameters.

$$N^{*} = \frac{D}{Q_{0}} = \frac{20000}{707.11} \approx 28.28 \text{ orders/year}$$

$$T^{*} = \frac{Q_{0}}{D} = \frac{707.11}{20000} \approx 0.0354 \text{ years} \approx 12.93 \text{ days}$$

$$\text{TVC} = \frac{20000}{707.11}\cdot 150 + \frac{707.11}{2}\cdot 12 + 20000 \times 40$$

$$= 4242.64 + 4242.64 + 800000 = 808485.28 \text{ ₹/year}$$

### Part (b) — Constrained Re-derivation (for illustration, even though constraint is non-binding)

**Lagrangian:**

$$L(Q, \lambda) = \frac{20000 \times 150}{Q} + \frac{Q \times 12}{2} + \lambda(2000 - 2Q)$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial Q} = -\frac{3{,}000{,}000}{Q^{2}} + 6 - 2\lambda = 0 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \lambda} = 2000 - 2Q = 0 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

From (ii): $Q = 1000$ units. Substituting in (i):

$$-\frac{3{,}000{,}000}{10^{6}} + 6 - 2\lambda = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; -3 + 6 - 2\lambda = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda = 1.5$$

Since $\lambda = 1.5 > 0$, the constraint would be active if we *forced* it. However, since the unconstrained optimum (707.11) already satisfies the constraint, the **shadow price is logically zero** — adding space doesn't help because we don't need it. **Optimal answer remains Q* = 707.11 units, λ = 0, N* ≈ 28.28 orders/year, TVC ≈ ₹8,08,485/yr.** (Full marks: 7)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Capital-Constrained EOQ (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**
>
> An electronics retailer has annual demand of **8,000 units** of a smartphone accessory. Ordering cost is **₹100 per order**, carrying cost is **₹8 per unit per year**, and unit price is **₹250**. The retailer's working-capital limit allows him to invest at most **₹50,000** in average inventory.
>
> **(a)** Compute the classical EOQ and verify the capital constraint. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Find the optimal order quantity under the capital constraint and the minimum total cost. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Given:** D = 8000 units, O = ₹100, C = ₹8/unit/yr, P = ₹250/unit, A = ₹50,000 (capital).

### Part (a) — Classical EOQ & Constraint Check

**Step 1.** Classical EOQ.

$$Q_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 8000 \cdot 100}{8}} = \sqrt{200000} \approx 447.21 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2.** Capital tied up at classical EOQ.

$$\frac{Q_{0} \cdot P}{2} = \frac{447.21 \times 250}{2} = 55898.44 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 3.** Compare with A = ₹50,000.

$$55898.44 \text{ ₹} \; > \; 50000 \text{ ₹} = A$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Capital constraint is **violated**. The retailer cannot afford the classical optimum. Lagrange / boundary method required.

### Part (b) — Optimal Constrained Order Quantity

**Step 1.** Maximum Q allowed by capital:

$$Q_{max} = \frac{2A}{P} = \frac{2 \times 50000}{250} = 400 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2.** Use Lagrange method to verify.

$$L(Q, \lambda) = \frac{8000 \times 100}{Q} + \frac{Q \times 8}{2} + \lambda(50000 - 250Q)$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial Q} = -\frac{800000}{Q^{2}} + 4 - 250\lambda = 0 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\lambda} = 50000 - 250Q = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = 200 \text{ units}$$

Substitute Q = 200 into (i):

$$-\frac{800000}{40000} + 4 - 250\lambda = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; -20 + 4 - 250\lambda = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda = \frac{-16}{250} < 0$$

**Negative λ indicates infeasibility** at Q = 200 under forced constraint. This means the boundary value $Q = 400$ is the feasible optimum.

**Step 3.** Verify by checking at $Q = Q_{max} = 400$:

$$\text{Ordering Cost} = \frac{8000}{400} \times 100 = 2000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\text{Carrying Cost} = \frac{400}{2} \times 8 = 1600 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\text{Purchase Cost} = 8000 \times 250 = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\text{Capital used} = \frac{400 \times 250}{2} = 50000 \text{ ₹ (exactly at the cap)}$$

**Step 4.** Confirm minimum cost by comparing with a slightly lower Q (sensitivity check):

At $Q = 380$: Ordering = 2105.26, Carrying = 1520 → Total variable = 3625.26 ₹.
At $Q = 400$: Total variable = 3600 ₹.
At $Q = 420$: Ordering = 1904.76, Carrying = 1680 → Total variable = 3584.76 ₹, but capital used = 52,500 ₹ > 50,000 (infeasible).

**Conclusion:** $Q^{*} = 400$ units is optimal (3 marks).

**Step 5.** Final results (3 marks):

$$Q^{*} = 400 \text{ units}, \quad N^{*} = \frac{8000}{400} = 20 \text{ orders/year}$$

$$T^{*} = \frac{400}{8000} = 0.05 \text{ years} = 18.25 \text{ days}$$

$$\boxed{\text{TVC}_{\min} = 2000 + 1600 + 2{,}000{,}000 = 2{,}003{,}600 \text{ ₹/year}}$$

$$\text{Shadow price} \lambda \approx 0.064 \text{ ₹/₹ of additional capital}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Classical EOQ formula:** $Q_0 = \sqrt{2DO/C}$ — always compute this *first* (1 mark reserved for this step in 14-mark questions).
- **Constraint check is mandatory:** Plug $Q_0$ into the constraint. If satisfied → constraint is *non-binding*, **stop here**, λ = 0.
- **Storage constraint equation:** $q \cdot Q \leq W$; iterate λ in $Q = \sqrt{2DO/(C + 2\lambda q)}$ until $qQ = W$.
- **Capital constraint equation:** $Q \cdot P/2 \leq A$; use boundary $Q^* = 2A/P$ as a first check, refine with Lagrange.
- **KKT complementary slackness:** $\lambda \cdot (W - qQ) = 0$. If $\lambda > 0$, constraint is *active* (binding).
- **Shadow price interpretation:** λ is the rate of TVC reduction per unit of additional scarce resource — a powerful expansion-vs-rent decision tool.
- **Always report:** $Q^*, N^*, T^*, \text{TVC}^*, \lambda$ with **correct units**.
- **Negative λ in your calculation** = the assumed boundary is infeasible; reconsider the constraint direction.
- **Purchasing cost** $D \cdot P$ is constant w.r.t. Q and **can be dropped** during minimisation, but must be added back when reporting total cost.
- **Dimensional check:** Every term inside $\sqrt{}$ must yield *units²*. EOQ = $\sqrt{(\text{units} \cdot \text{₹}) / (\text{₹/unit})} = \text{units}$ ✓.
- **KTU Mark Distribution Pattern:** Part (a) of a 14-mark question typically tests the constraint verification (7 marks). Part (b) tests the Lagrange iteration and managerial interpretation (7 marks). Always keep these two halves clearly separated in your answer sheet.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: All-Units Quantity Discounts Model and Single Period Probabilistic Inventory Models with Discrete and Continuous Demand
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT862 - OPERATIONAL RESEARCH AND INDUSTRIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** All-Units Quantity Discounts Model and Single Period Probabilistic Inventory Models with Discrete and Continuous Demand

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor — Module 4 (Inventory Theory)**
> This module carries the highest KTU weightage for inventory modeling questions. Mastery of deterministic EOQ extensions (price-break models) and the stochastic single-period model is **mandatory** for ESE Part B (14-mark) questions.

---

### 1.1 All-Units Quantity Discounts Model (Price-Break EOQ)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU Text)**
> The *All-Units Quantity Discounts Model* is a deterministic inventory model in which the supplier offers a **reduced unit purchase price** when the buyer commits to ordering within a specific quantity range. *Crucially*, under an **all-units discount**, the lower price applies to **every unit** in the order (not just the incremental units above the break). The buyer must select an order quantity that **minimizes the Total Annual Cost (TAC)**, which is the sum of purchase cost, ordering cost, and holding cost.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Wholesale Mango Vendor 🛒**
Imagine you go to a wholesale fruit market. The vendor says:
- 1–9 kg → **Rs. 100/kg**
- 10–49 kg → **Rs. 90/kg** (cheaper for everyone if you buy 10+)
- 50 kg and above → **Rs. 80/kg**

If you buy exactly 50 kg, **all 50 kg** are billed at Rs. 80 each — you don't pay Rs. 90 for the first 49 and Rs. 80 for the 50th. That is the *all-units* structure. Now, your decision is **not** simply "buy more to save more," because every extra mango also increases your storage/holding cost. You must mathematically find the **sweet-spot quantity** that minimizes **Total Cost (Purchase + Ordering + Holding)**.

**Standard Cost Components (Industry Metrics):**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $D$ | Annual demand | units/year |
| $S$ | Ordering cost per order | Rs./order |
| $C$ | Unit purchase cost | Rs./unit |
| $i$ | Carrying charge fraction (interest, insurance, obsolescence) | dimensionless (e.g., 0.20) |
| $H = i \cdot C$ | Holding cost per unit per year | Rs./unit/year |
| $Q$ | Order quantity (decision variable) | units |

---

### 1.2 Single-Period Probabilistic Inventory Model (Newsvendor Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU Text)**
> The *Single-Period Probabilistic Inventory Model* addresses items with a **fixed single selling season** and **uncertain (random) demand**. Demand is described by a probability distribution. The objective is to determine the order quantity $Q^*$ that **maximizes Expected Profit** (or **minimizes Expected Cost**) by balancing the cost of stocking too much (overage) against the cost of stocking too little (underage).

**Intuitive Analogy — The Newspaper Stall 📰**
A newsboy buys newspapers every morning at Rs. 12 each and sells them at Rs. 20. Unsold papers are returned (or scrapped) for Rs. 5 salvage. Demand tomorrow is unknown — sometimes 70 papers sell, sometimes 90. Order too many → waste money on leftovers. Order too few → lose profit from missed sales. The **optimal order** is found by balancing these two regrets, expressed mathematically as a **critical ratio** $P(D \le Q^*) \ge \dfrac{C_u}{C_u + C_o}$.

**Core Cost Symmetry:**

| Cost Type | Symbol | Definition | In Newsvendor Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Underage Cost** | $C_u$ | Profit lost per unit of unmet demand | $20 - 12 = \mathbf{8}$ |
| **Overage Cost** | $C_o$ | Loss per unit of leftover inventory | $12 - 5 = \mathbf{7}$ |
| **Critical Ratio** | $P$ | $\dfrac{C_u}{C_u + C_o}$ | $\dfrac{8}{15} = 0.5333$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Demand Variants Covered by KTU 2024:**
> 1. **Discrete Demand** — demand takes specific integer values (e.g., 50, 60, 70) with known probabilities.
> 2. **Continuous Demand** — demand follows a continuous distribution (commonly **Normal**, **Uniform**, or **Exponential**).
>
> The mathematical machinery differs slightly — discrete uses cumulative probability tables; continuous uses the **inverse CDF** (quantile function).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — All-Units Quantity Discounts

The **Total Annual Cost (TAC)** function for any given unit cost $C$ and order quantity $Q$ is:

$$
TAC(Q, C) \;=\; \underbrace{D \cdot C}_{\text{Purchase}} \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} \cdot S}_{\text{Ordering}} \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} \cdot H}_{\text{Holding}} \;=\; D \cdot C \;+\; \frac{D \cdot S}{Q} \;+\; \frac{Q \cdot i \cdot C}{2}
$$

**The "Why" Behind Each Term:**
- $D \cdot C$ → Money paid to supplier (increases linearly with quantity ordered over the year).
- $\frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$ → Number of orders/year $\times$ cost per order; decreases as $Q$ grows (fewer, larger orders).
- $\frac{Q}{2} \cdot H$ → Average inventory $\times$ holding cost/unit; increases as $Q$ grows.

**The Classical EOQ (Wilson Formula)** — derived by setting $\frac{dTAC}{dQ} = 0$:

$$
Q^* \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{i \cdot C}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{H}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Discount Algorithm — Why Naive Cheapest-Price Thinking Fails**
> When price drops with quantity, the **holding cost $H = iC$ also drops** (because $C$ drops). This means the EOQ itself *shifts upward* as price falls — but not necessarily enough to be feasible in that price bracket. Therefore, KTU expects the **step-by-step algorithm** below — students who skip it lose 4–5 marks.

**KTU-Standard Algorithm for All-Units Discounts (Mandatory Procedure):**

> **Step 1:** Sort price breaks from **lowest to highest price** (i.e., largest discount first).
>
> **Step 2:** For the lowest price, compute EOQ. If EOQ lies within that bracket → feasible; record its TAC. If not feasible → set $Q$ to the **upper limit of the bracket**, compute TAC.
>
> **Step 3:** Move to the next higher price. Recompute EOQ. Check feasibility. Record TAC.
>
> **Step 4:** Repeat until you finish the highest-price bracket (whose EOQ is **always** feasible, since it is the smallest price bracket).
>
> **Step 5:** Compare all feasible TAC values. **The minimum TAC wins** — its corresponding $Q$ is optimal.

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- **Procurement & Supply Chain:** Procurement managers in automotive, pharma, and FMCG industries use this model weekly to negotiate bulk contracts.
- **Warehouse Sizing:** Determines whether storage capacity can support the bulk order.
- **Cash Flow Management:** Avoids tying up excess capital in inventory.

---

### 2.2 Theoretical Foundations — Single-Period Probabilistic Model

Let demand $D$ be a random variable with PMF $P(D=d)$ (discrete) or PDF $f(d)$ (continuous), and let the order quantity be $Q$.

**Profit/Loss Structure per Realization:**

If demand $D \ge Q$ → sell all $Q$, profit = $Q \cdot (p - c) - (D - Q) \cdot C_u$ is the lost-opportunity regret.

If demand $D < Q$ → sell only $D$, leftover = $Q - D$, profit = $D \cdot (p - c) - (Q - D) \cdot C_o$.

**Expected Profit Maximization (Equivalent to Cost Minimization):**

$$
E[\text{Profit}(Q)] \;=\; \sum_{d=0}^{\infty} \pi(d) \cdot \text{Profit}(Q, d)
$$

Setting $\frac{\Delta E[\text{Profit}]}{\Delta Q} = 0$ yields the famous **Critical Ratio (Newsvendor Critical Fractile):**

$$
\boxed{\; P(D \le Q^*) \;\ge\; \dfrac{C_u}{C_u + C_o} \;}
$$

**Interpretation:** Order enough stock so that the **probability of meeting demand** is at least the critical ratio. This is also called the **optimal service level**.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Model / Concept | Governing Equation | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Annual Cost (TAC) | $TAC = D \cdot C + \dfrac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \dfrac{Q \cdot i \cdot C}{2}$ | All $Q, C > 0$ |
| 2 | Wilson EOQ | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{i C}}$ | Applies only when $Q^*$ falls in feasible price range |
| 3 | Holding Cost Coefficient | $H = i \cdot C$ | $i \in [0,1]$ typically |
| 4 | Feasibility Check | $Q_{\min}(j) \le Q^* \le Q_{\max}(j)$ | Bracket $j$ |
| 5 | Infeasible Bracket Fix | Set $Q = Q_{\max}(j)$ | Only when $Q^* < Q_{\min}(j)$ or $Q^* > Q_{\max}(j)$ |
| 6 | Underage Cost | $C_u = p - c$ | $p$ = selling price, $c$ = unit cost |
| 7 | Overage Cost | $C_o = c - s$ | $s$ = salvage value |
| 8 | Critical Ratio (Service Level) | $\dfrac{C_u}{C_u + C_o}$ | Always $\in (0, 1)$ |
| 9 | Optimality Condition | $P(D \le Q^*) \ge \dfrac{C_u}{C_u + C_o}$ | Newsvendor optimality |
| 10 | Discrete Solution Rule | Smallest $Q$ s.t. $F(Q) \ge \dfrac{C_u}{C_u + C_o}$ | $F$ = CDF |
| 11 | Continuous Solution Rule | $Q^* = F^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{C_u}{C_u + C_o}\right)$ | For Normal: $Q^* = \mu + z \cdot \sigma$ |
| 12 | Z-value (Standard Normal) | $z = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{C_u}{C_u + C_o}\right)$ | From standard normal tables |
| 13 | Expected Profit (Discrete) | $E[\pi(Q)] = \sum_d P(D=d) \cdot \pi(Q, d)$ | Sum over all $d$ |
| 14 | Expected Profit (Continuous) | $E[\pi(Q)] = \int_{0}^{\infty} \pi(Q, x) f(x) \, dx$ | Integral over demand domain |

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award full marks only if you (a) state the critical ratio explicitly, (b) use cumulative probability correctly, and (c) verify optimality by checking **bracketing values** $Q-1$ and $Q+1$ around the optimum in discrete demand.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Probabilistic Single-Period Models

- **Fashion Retail:** Seasonal garments must be ordered once; demand uncertain.
- **Perishable Goods:** Bakery items, fresh produce, flowers, daily newspapers.
- **Capital Goods with End-of-Life:** Spare parts for soon-to-be-discontinued models.
- **Holiday/Event Merchandise:** Christmas decorations, Diwali specials — ordering happens **once**, after which no replenishment.
- **Defense Procurement:** Perishable munitions, rations for a fixed mission window.
- **Aerospace/Automotive Launch Inventory:** Initial spare-part stocking for a newly launched model with uncertain service demand.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — All-Units Quantity Discounts

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern, Adapted]**

**Problem Statement:**
A retailer purchases a component with annual demand $D = 5000$ units. Ordering cost $S = \text{Rs. } 200$ per order. Holding cost fraction $i = 0.20$ (20% of unit cost). The supplier offers an **all-units** discount schedule:

| Quantity Bracket (units) | Unit Price (Rs.) |
|---|---|
| 0 – 999 | **25** |
| 1000 – 1999 | **24** |
| 2000 and above | **23** |

Determine the **optimal order quantity** and **minimum Total Annual Cost**.

---

#### Step 1 — Organize the Brackets (Sorted Lowest Price First)

Working from cheapest price upward ensures monotonic analysis. Brackets (low → high price):

| Bracket $j$ | Range | Unit Price $C_j$ | Holding $H_j = 0.20 \cdot C_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (cheapest) | 2000+ | Rs. 23 | Rs. 4.60 |
| 2 | 1000 – 1999 | Rs. 24 | Rs. 4.80 |
| 3 (most expensive) | 0 – 999 | Rs. 25 | Rs. 5.00 |

---

#### Step 2 — Compute EOQ for Bracket 1 ($C = 23$)

$$
Q_1^* \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{i \cdot C_1}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times 200}{0.20 \times 23}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2{,}000{,}000}{4.6}} \;=\; \sqrt{434{,}782.61}
$$

$$
Q_1^* \;\approx\; 659.4 \text{ units}
$$

**Feasibility Check:** Is $659.4 \in [2000, \infty)$? **No.** EOQ lies in Bracket 3's territory.

**Action:** Since $Q_1^* < Q_{\min}(1) = 2000$, we **cannot** order at Bracket 1's price. Set $Q = Q_{\max}(1)$ — but actually, in the all-units algorithm, we **proceed to the next higher price**. We will revisit Bracket 1 *only* if it becomes feasible at a later stage (it won't here). Record no feasible TAC for Bracket 1 yet.

---

#### Step 3 — Compute EOQ for Bracket 2 ($C = 24$)

$$
Q_2^* \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 5000 \cdot 200}{0.20 \times 24}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2{,}000{,}000}{4.8}} \;=\; \sqrt{416{,}666.67} \;\approx\; 645.5 \text{ units}
$$

**Feasibility Check:** Is $645.5 \in [1000, 1999]$? **No.** Infeasible.

**Action:** This bracket is infeasible too. We must use it only if the next (higher) bracket forces us to evaluate its boundary. Skip for now.

---

#### Step 4 — Compute EOQ for Bracket 3 ($C = 25$, highest price)

$$
Q_3^* \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 5000 \cdot 200}{0.20 \times 25}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2{,}000{,}000}{5.0}} \;=\; \sqrt{400{,}000} \;=\; 632.46 \text{ units}
$$

**Feasibility Check:** Is $632.46 \in [0, 999]$? **YES.** Feasible.

Compute TAC at $Q = 632.46$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAC_3 \;&=\; D \cdot C_3 \;+\; \frac{D \cdot S}{Q_3^*} \;+\; \frac{Q_3^* \cdot i \cdot C_3}{2} \\[4pt]
&=\; 5000 \times 25 \;+\; \frac{5000 \times 200}{632.46} \;+\; \frac{632.46 \times 0.20 \times 25}{2} \\[4pt]
&=\; 125{,}000 \;+\; \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{632.46} \;+\; \frac{3162.30}{2} \\[4pt]
&=\; 125{,}000 \;+\; 1581.14 \;+\; 1581.15 \\[4pt]
&\approx\; \mathbf{Rs. \; 128{,}162.29}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 5 — Backward Pass (The Crucial KTU Step) ⭐

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why we now go back to cheaper brackets:**
> Even though $Q_1^*$ and $Q_2^*$ were infeasible as optimal EOQ values, **buying more to get a discount is still worth checking** — because a bulk order at a lower price may yield a *lower* TAC than buying 632 units at the highest price.

**Evaluate Bracket 2 at its minimum order quantity $Q = 1000$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAC_2 \;&=\; 5000 \times 24 \;+\; \frac{5000 \times 200}{1000} \;+\; \frac{1000 \times 0.20 \times 24}{2} \\[4pt]
&=\; 120{,}000 \;+\; 1000 \;+\; 2400 \;=\; \mathbf{Rs. \; 123{,}400}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Evaluate Bracket 1 at its minimum order quantity $Q = 2000$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAC_1 \;&=\; 5000 \times 23 \;+\; \frac{5000 \times 200}{2000} \;+\; \frac{2000 \times 0.20 \times 23}{2} \\[4pt]
&=\; 115{,}000 \;+\; 500 \;+\; 4600 \;=\; \mathbf{Rs. \; 120{,}100}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 6 — Decision Matrix

| Bracket | $C$ (Rs.) | Order $Q$ (units) | $TAC$ (Rs./year) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3 | 25 | 632 | 128,162 | Feasible EOQ |
| 2 | 24 | 1000 | **123,400** | Boundary quantity |
| 1 | 23 | 2000 | **120,100** ✅ | **OPTIMAL** |

**Conclusion:** $Q^* = \mathbf{2000}$ units, with $TAC^* = \mathbf{Rs. \; 120{,}100}$ per year.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Learning:** Buying 2000 units (which is **3.16×** the natural EOQ) saves **Rs. 8,062/year** compared to ordering at the natural EOQ of 632. The discount outweighs the holding penalty.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Worked Example — Single-Period, **Discrete** Demand

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14-mark pattern sub-part]**

**Problem Statement:**
A bakery produces fresh cupcakes every morning at $c = \text{Rs. } 30$/unit and sells at $p = \text{Rs. } 50$/unit. Unsold cupcakes are donated (salvage) for $s = \text{Rs. } 10$/unit tax-deductible value. Daily demand $D$ has the following distribution:

| Demand $d$ (units) | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 | 70 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P(D = d)$ | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.30 | 0.25 | 0.15 |

Determine the optimal order quantity $Q^*$ and the corresponding expected profit.

---

#### Step 1 — Compute Underage and Overage Costs

$$
C_u \;=\; p - c \;=\; 50 - 30 \;=\; \mathbf{Rs. \; 20}
$$

$$
C_o \;=\; c - s \;=\; 30 - 10 \;=\; \mathbf{Rs. \; 20}
$$

---

#### Step 2 — Compute the Critical Ratio (Service Level)

$$
\text{Critical Ratio} \;=\; \frac{C_u}{C_u + C_o} \;=\; \frac{20}{20 + 20} \;=\; \frac{20}{40} \;=\; \mathbf{0.50}
$$

---

#### Step 3 — Build the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(30) \;&=\; P(D \le 30) \;=\; 0.10 \\
F(40) \;&=\; P(D \le 40) \;=\; 0.10 + 0.20 \;=\; 0.30 \\
F(50) \;&=\; P(D \le 50) \;=\; 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.30 \;=\; 0.70 \\
F(60) \;&=\; P(D \le 60) \;=\; 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.25 \;=\; 0.85 \\
F(70) \;&=\; P(D \le 70) \;=\; 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.15 \;=\; 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 4 — Apply the Optimality Condition

We seek the **smallest $Q$** such that $F(Q) \ge 0.50$:

| $Q$ | $F(Q)$ | $F(Q) \ge 0.50$? |
|---|---|---|
| 30 | 0.10 | ❌ |
| 40 | 0.30 | ❌ |
| **50** | **0.70** | ✅ |
| 60 | 0.85 | ✅ |
| 70 | 1.00 | ✅ |

Therefore: $\boxed{Q^* = 50 \text{ cupcakes}}$

---

#### Step 5 — Verify with Expected Profit (KTU Full-Marks Requirement)

Profit for each demand realization when ordering $Q = 50$:

$$
\text{Profit}(Q, d) \;=\; \min(Q, d) \cdot (p - c) \;-\; \max(0, Q - d) \cdot (c - s)
$$

| Demand $d$ | Sold $\min(Q,d)$ | Leftover $Q-d$ | Profit (Rs.) | $P(D=d)$ | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 30 | 30 | 20 | $30(20) - 20(20) = 200$ | 0.10 | 20 |
| 40 | 40 | 10 | $40(20) - 10(20) = 600$ | 0.20 | 120 |
| **50** | **50** | **0** | $50(20) - 0 = 1000$ | **0.30** | **300** |
| 60 | 50 | 0 | $50(20) - 0 = 1000$ | 0.25 | 250 |
| 70 | 50 | 0 | $50(20) - 0 = 1000$ | 0.15 | 150 |

$$
E[\pi(50)] \;=\; 20 + 120 + 300 + 250 + 150 \;=\; \mathbf{Rs. \; 840}
$$

**Verify with neighbors ($Q = 40$ and $Q = 60$) — a KTU mandatory step:**

For $Q = 40$:
- $d=30$: $30(20) - 10(20) = 400$ → $0.10 \times 400 = 40$
- $d=40$: $40(20) = 800$ → $0.20 \times 800 = 160$
- $d=50,60,70$: sold=40 each → $40(20) = 800$ each → $(0.30+0.25+0.15)\times 800 = 560$
- Total: $E[\pi(40)] = 40 + 160 + 560 = \mathbf{Rs. \; 760}$

For $Q = 60$:
- $d=30$: $30(20) - 30(20) = 0$ → $0$
- $d=40$: $40(20) - 20(20) = 400$ → $0.20 \times 400 = 80$
- $d=50$: $50(20) - 10(20) = 800$ → $0.30 \times 800 = 240$
- $d=60,70$: sold=60 → $60(20)=1200$ each → $(0.25+0.15)\times 1200 = 480$
- Total: $E[\pi(60)] = 0 + 80 + 240 + 480 = \mathbf{Rs. \; 800}$

| $Q$ | 40 | **50** | 60 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E[\pi(Q)]$ | 760 | **840** ✅ | 800 |

**Conclusion verified:** $Q^* = 50$, $E[\pi^*] = \text{Rs. } 840$.

---

### 3.3 Detailed Worked Example — Single-Period, **Continuous** Demand

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern, Adapted]**

**Problem Statement:**
A vendor stocks umbrellas for the monsoon season. Unit cost $c = \text{Rs. } 100$, selling price $p = \text{Rs. } 200$, salvage value $s = \text{Rs. } 40$. Demand $D$ is **normally distributed** with mean $\mu = 500$ umbrellas and standard deviation $\sigma = 80$. Find the optimal order quantity.

---

#### Step 1 — Compute Underage and Overage Costs

$$
C_u \;=\; p - c \;=\; 200 - 100 \;=\; \mathbf{Rs. \; 100}
$$

$$
C_o \;=\; c - s \;=\; 100 - 40 \;=\; \mathbf{Rs. \; 60}
$$

---

#### Step 2 — Critical Ratio

$$
\frac{C_u}{C_u + C_o} \;=\; \frac{100}{100 + 60} \;=\; \frac{100}{160} \;=\; 0.625
$$

---

#### Step 3 — Find the Z-Value from Standard Normal Table

We need $z$ such that $\Phi(z) = 0.625$:

$$
\Phi(0.32) \;\approx\; 0.6255 \quad \Rightarrow \quad z \;\approx\; \mathbf{0.32}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Normal Table Reference (KTU allowed):** $\Phi(0.32) = 0.6255$, $\Phi(0.31) = 0.6217$. Interpolating: $z = 0.31 + \dfrac{0.625 - 0.6217}{0.6255 - 0.6217} \times 0.01 = 0.31 + 0.87 \times 0.01 \approx 0.319 \approx 0.32$.

---

#### Step 4 — Apply the Continuous Inverse CDF Rule

$$
Q^* \;=\; \mu \;+\; z \cdot \sigma \;=\; 500 \;+\; (0.32)(80) \;=\; 500 \;+\; 25.6 \;=\; \mathbf{525.6 \;\approx\; 526 \text{ umbrellas}}
$$

---

#### Step 5 — Interpretation

- Order **526 umbrellas**.
- Probability of selling out or stocking out:
  - $P(\text{stockout}) = P(D > Q^*) = 1 - 0.625 = 0.375$ → **37.5%** chance of unmet demand.
  - $P(\text{no stockout}) = P(D \le Q^*) = 0.625$ → **62.5% service level**.

---

### 3.4 Summary Table — Algorithm Comparison

| Aspect | Discrete Demand | Continuous Demand |
|---|---|---|
| Demand description | $P(D = d_i)$ for specific $d_i$ | PDF $f(d)$ or CDF $F(d)$ |
| Optimality rule | Smallest $Q$ where $F(Q) \ge \dfrac{C_u}{C_u + C_o}$ | $Q^* = F^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{C_u}{C_u + C_o}\right)$ |
| Tool needed | Cumulative probability table | Inverse CDF (quantile function) |
| Common distributions | Poisson, empirical PMF, custom tables | Normal, Exponential, Uniform |
| Verification step | Compute $E[\pi(Q^*-1)], E[\pi(Q^*)], E[\pi(Q^*+1)]$ | Compare $E[\pi]$ for $Q^* \pm \Delta$ |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — All-Units Quantity Discount Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Gather D, S, i, Discount Schedule] --> B[Sort price breaks<br>from lowest to highest price]
    B --> C[Initialize empty TAC list]
    C --> D[For each price bracket j]
    D --> E[Compute EOQ at price C sub j<br>Q star equals sqrt 2 D S over i C sub j]
    E --> F{Is Q star within<br>bracket j range?}
    F -- Yes Feasible --> G[Set Q equals Q star<br>Compute TAC sub j]
    F -- No Infeasible --> H[Set Q equals upper bound<br>of bracket j for TAC]
    G --> I[Store TAC sub j in list]
    H --> I
    I --> J{More brackets<br>remaining?}
    J -- Yes --> D
    J -- No --> K[Select minimum TAC<br>from list]
    K --> L[Output optimal Q star<br>and TAC star]
    L --> M[End]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Newsvendor (Single-Period Probabilistic) Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given p, c, s<br>and demand distribution] --> B[Compute Cu equals p minus c]
    B --> C[Compute Co equals c minus s]
    C --> D[Compute Critical Ratio<br>CR equals Cu over Cu plus Co]
    D --> E{Demand type?}
    E -- Discrete --> F[Build CDF F of d<br>using cumulative sums]
    F --> G[Find smallest Q<br>where F of Q greater or equal CR]
    G --> H[Verify with neighbors<br>compute E profit at Q minus 1, Q, Q plus 1]
    H --> I[Optimal Q star found<br>report expected profit]
    E -- Continuous --> J[Find z such that<br>Phi of z equals CR<br>from standard normal table]
    J --> K[Compute Q star equals mu plus z times sigma]
    K --> L[Report service level<br>equals CR and stockout risk<br>equals 1 minus CR]
    L --> I
    I --> M[End]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cost Component Decomposition for TAC

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TAC[Total Annual Cost TAC]
        PURCH[Purchase Cost equals D times C]
        ORDER[Ordering Cost equals D times S over Q]
        HOLD[Holding Cost equals Q times i times C over 2]
    end
    PURCH -- Linear in C --> TAC
    ORDER -- Decreases as Q grows --> TAC
    HOLD -- Increases as Q grows --> TAC
    TAC -- Minimum at EOQ --> OPT[Optimal Q star]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Demand-Type Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Single-Period Inventory Model] --> T{Demand Nature}
    T -- Certain and known --> D1[Deterministic<br>EOQ with Discount]
    T -- Random --> D2[Probabilistic<br>Newsvendor]
    D2 --> V{Probability type}
    V -- Discrete values --> D3[Use CDF table<br>smallest Q with F of Q greater or equal CR]
    V -- Continuous --> D4[Use inverse CDF<br>Q star equals F inverse of CR]
    D4 --> D5[Normal: Q star equals mu plus z sigma]
    D4 --> D6[Exponential: Q star equals minus ln of 1 minus CR over lambda]
```

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Part A]**
*(3 Marks)*
**"Differentiate between All-Units Discounts and Incremental-Unit Discounts in inventory models. Which one is more complex to analyze and why?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks allocation):**

| Aspect | All-Units Discount | Incremental Discount |
|---|---|---|
| Price applies to | **All** units in the lot | Only units **above** the break |
| Mathematical structure | Single unit cost $C$ per order | Piece-wise piecewise cost |
| Complexity | Moderate (algorithm-based) | Higher (multi-tier cost accounting) |
| Common in practice | Bulk retail, B2B | Tiered utility tariffs, quantity pricing |

**Marking:** 1.5 marks for tabular differentiation; 1.5 marks for explaining why incremental is more complex (because the cost function is piecewise, not a single EOQ).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Part A]**
*(3 Marks)*
**"State and explain the concept of the Critical Ratio in the Single-Period Probabilistic Inventory Model. What is its range of values?"**

**Model Answer:**
The **Critical Ratio** $\dfrac{C_u}{C_u + C_o}$ represents the optimal **in-stock probability** (service level) that the decision-maker should target.
- $C_u$ = underage cost (per-unit profit lost from a stockout).
- $C_o$ = overage cost (per-unit loss from leftover inventory).
- **Range:** $(0, 1)$ — strictly between 0 and 1.
- Higher $C_u$ (expensive stockouts) → higher critical ratio → order more.
- Higher $C_o$ (expensive leftovers) → lower critical ratio → order less.
- Equivalently, $P(D \le Q^*) \ge \dfrac{C_u}{C_u + C_o}$.

**Marking:** 1 mark for definition; 1 mark for formula; 1 mark for range and interpretation.

---
