### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Quantity Discounts + Newsvendor Discrete

**[KTU University Exam — University Question Pattern, Full Module]**

**(a)** A wholesaler purchases a product with annual demand $D = 10{,}000$ units. Ordering cost $S = \text{Rs. } 400$ per order. Inventory carrying cost is $i = 25\%$ of the unit price. The supplier offers an all-units discount schedule as follows:

| Quantity Ordered | Unit Price (Rs.) |
|---|---|
| 0 – 499 | 50 |
| 500 – 999 | 48 |
| 1000 and above | 45 |

Determine the **optimal order quantity** and the corresponding **minimum total annual cost**.
**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bookstore sells a newly released novel at $p = \text{Rs. } 300$ per copy. Cost $c = \text{Rs. } 180$. Unsold copies are returned to the publisher for $s = \text{Rs. } 100$ salvage. Demand distribution is:

| Demand $d$ | 100 | 150 | 200 | 250 | 300 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P(D=d)$ | 0.10 | 0.25 | 0.35 | 0.20 | 0.10 |

Determine the optimal number of copies to stock. Verify your answer by computing expected profit at $Q^*-1$, $Q^*$, and $Q^*+1$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Sort brackets (lowest price first):**

| Bracket | Range | $C$ | $H = 0.25 \cdot C$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1000+ | Rs. 45 | Rs. 11.25 |
| 2 | 500–999 | Rs. 48 | Rs. 12.00 |
| 3 | 0–499 | Rs. 50 | Rs. 12.50 |

**Step 2 — Compute EOQs (lowest to highest price):**

Bracket 1 ($C=45$):

$$
Q_1^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 400}{11.25}} = \sqrt{711{,}111.11} \approx 843.27
$$

Not feasible (need $Q \ge 1000$). Skip.

Bracket 2 ($C=48$):

$$
Q_2^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 400}{12.00}} = \sqrt{666{,}666.67} \approx 816.50
$$

Feasible ($Q \in [500, 999]$). Compute TAC:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAC_2 &= 10{,}000 \times 48 + \frac{10{,}000 \times 400}{816.50} + \frac{816.50 \times 12.00}{2} \\
&= 480{,}000 + 4899.05 + 4899.00 \\
&\approx \mathbf{Rs. \; 489{,}798.05}
\end{aligned}
$$

Bracket 3 ($C=50$):

$$
Q_3^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 400}{12.50}} = \sqrt{640{,}000} \approx 800.00
$$

Not feasible ($800 > 499$). Use boundary $Q = 499$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAC_3^{(499)} &= 10{,}000 \times 50 + \frac{10{,}000 \times 400}{499} + \frac{499 \times 12.50}{2} \\
&= 500{,}000 + 8016.03 + 3118.75 \\
&\approx \mathbf{Rs. \; 511{,}134.78}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Backward Pass — Check Bracket 1 at $Q = 1000$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAC_1^{(1000)} &= 10{,}000 \times 45 + \frac{10{,}000 \times 400}{1000} + \frac{1000 \times 11.25}{2} \\
&= 450{,}000 + 4000 + 5625 \\
&= \mathbf{Rs. \; 459{,}625}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision Matrix:**

| Bracket | $Q$ | TAC (Rs.) |
|---|---|---|
| 3 ($Q=499$) | 499 | 511,134.78 |
| 2 ($Q=816.50$) | 816 | 489,798.05 |
| 1 ($Q=1000$) | 1000 | **459,625.00** ✅ |

**Answer:** $Q^* = \mathbf{1000}$ units, $TAC^* = \mathbf{Rs. \; 459{,}625}$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Cost computations:**

$$
C_u = p - c = 300 - 180 = 120; \quad C_o = c - s = 180 - 100 = 80
$$

**Step 2 — Critical Ratio:**

$$
CR = \frac{120}{120 + 80} = \frac{120}{200} = 0.60
$$

**Step 3 — Build CDF:**

| $d$ | 100 | 150 | 200 | 250 | 300 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $F(d)$ | 0.10 | 0.35 | 0.70 | 0.90 | 1.00 |

Smallest $Q$ with $F(Q) \ge 0.60$ → **$Q^* = 200$**.

**Step 4 — Verification:**

Profit equation: $\pi(Q, d) = \min(Q,d)(p-c) - \max(0, Q-d)(c-s)$

| Demand $d$ | Profit at $Q=199$ | Profit at $Q=200$ | Profit at $Q=201$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 100 | $100(120)-99(80)=4080$ | $100(120)-100(80)=4000$ | $100(120)-101(80)=3920$ |
| 150 | $150(120)-49(80)=14080$ | $150(120)-50(80)=14000$ | $150(120)-51(80)=13920$ |
| 200 | $199(120)=23880$ | $200(120)=24000$ | $200(120)-1(80)=23920$ |
| 250 | $199(120)=23880$ | $200(120)=24000$ | $201(120)=24120$ |
| 300 | $199(120)=23880$ | $200(120)=24000$ | $201(120)=24120$ |

**Expected profits:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\pi(199)] &= 0.10(4080) + 0.25(14080) + 0.35(23880) + 0.20(23880) + 0.10(23880) \\
&= 408 + 3520 + 8358 + 4776 + 2388 = 19{,}450 \\
E[\pi(200)] &= 0.10(4000) + 0.25(14000) + 0.35(24000) + 0.20(24000) + 0.10(24000) \\
&= 400 + 3500 + 8400 + 4800 + 2400 = \mathbf{19{,}500} \; ✅ \\
E[\pi(201)] &= 0.10(3920) + 0.25(13920) + 0.35(23920) + 0.20(24120) + 0.10(24120) \\
&= 392 + 3480 + 8372 + 4824 + 2412 = 19{,}480
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $Q^* = 200$ copies, $E[\pi^*] = \mathbf{Rs. \; 19{,}500}$.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Continuous Demand Newsvendor + Concept

**(a)** A pharmaceutical company stocks a flu vaccine for a single season. Cost $c = \text{Rs. } 250$, price $p = \text{Rs. } 400$, salvage $s = \text{Rs. } 100$. Demand is normally distributed with $\mu = 1000$ doses and $\sigma = 150$ doses. Compute:
- (i) The critical ratio.
- (ii) The optimal order quantity $Q^*$.
- (iii) The expected profit at $Q^*$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a numerical illustration why, in the All-Units Quantity Discount Model, the buyer must consider order quantities that are *not* the natural EOQ. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**(i) Critical Ratio:**

$$
C_u = 400 - 250 = 150, \quad C_o = 250 - 100 = 150
$$

$$
CR = \frac{150}{150 + 150} = 0.50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad z = 0
$$

**(ii) Optimal Quantity:**

$$
Q^* = \mu + z\sigma = 1000 + 0 \times 150 = \mathbf{1000 \text{ doses}}
$$

**(iii) Expected Profit at $Q^* = 1000$:**

Since $z = 0$, exactly 50% stockout probability. Standard expected profit formula for normal demand:

$$
E[\pi^*] \;=\; (p - c)\mu \;-\; (C_o)(Q^* - \mu + L(z)\sigma)
$$

where $L(z) = \phi(z) - z(1 - \Phi(z))$ is the standard normal loss function.
At $z=0$: $\phi(0) = 0.3989$, $1 - \Phi(0) = 0.5$

$$
L(0) = 0.3989 - 0(0.5) = 0.3989
$$

$$
E[\pi^*] = (150)(1000) - (150)(0 + 0.3989 \times 150) = 150{,}000 - 8975.25 = \mathbf{Rs. \; 141{,}024.75}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Conceptual (7 Marks)

In the All-Units Quantity Discount Model, the **natural EOQ** computed at a given price may fall **outside** that price's feasible bracket. For example, with $D=5000$, $S=200$, $i=0.20$, and $C=23$:

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times 200}{0.20 \times 23}} \approx 659.4
$$

But the bracket requiring $C=23$ mandates $Q \ge 2000$. Therefore $Q^*$ is **infeasible** at that price. The decision-maker must evaluate the TAC at the **boundary quantity** $Q=2000$ and compare it with TAC at the feasible EOQ of the next-higher price bracket. In our earlier worked example, $TAC(2000) = 120{,}100$ was less than $TAC(632) = 128{,}162$ — proving that **bulk orders at lower prices can beat the natural EOQ**. Ignoring this and ordering only at the EOQ would **cost the firm Rs. 8,062/year unnecessarily**. *(7 marks: 2 marks concept + 2 marks numerical illustration + 2 marks comparison + 1 mark conclusion)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Skipping the backward pass:** Many students compute EOQ for the lowest-price bracket, find it infeasible, and **stop** — losing 4 marks. You MUST check the boundary quantity against the next-higher bracket's TAC.
>
> 2. **Forgetting $H = i \cdot C$:** When $C$ changes across brackets, $H$ **also changes**. Using a single $H$ for all brackets yields wrong TAC and is a 3-mark penalty.
>
> 3. **Newsboy discrete — wrong smallest $Q$:** KTU requires the **smallest** $Q$ satisfying $F(Q) \ge CR$, not just any $Q$. Off-by-one errors cost 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Newsboy continuous — forgetting Z-table interpolation:** At $CR = 0.625$, simply writing $z \approx 0.32$ is acceptable only if interpolation is shown. Writing "from table" without value = 1 mark lost.
>
> 5. **Not verifying with neighbors in discrete demand:** Always compute $E[\pi(Q^*-1)]$, $E[\pi(Q^*)]$, $E[\pi(Q^*+1)]$ to prove optimality — this fetches the full **3 marks** of the verification step.
>
> 6. **Confusing $C_u$ and $C_o$:** Underage = profit lost per missed sale (selling price − cost). Overage = cost of leftover (cost − salvage). Swapped symbols = entire answer wrong.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Module 4 — Price-Breaks & Single-Period Probabilistic Models**

### 🔹 All-Units Quantity Discounts

- [ ] **TAC = $D \cdot C + (D/Q) \cdot S + (Q/2) \cdot i \cdot C$** — memorize the three components.
- [ ] EOQ formula: $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/(iC)}$.
- [ ] Holding cost **recomputes** for each price bracket: $H = i \cdot C_j$.
- [ ] **Algorithm rule:** Sort prices low → high. Compute EOQ per bracket. If feasible, use it; if not, evaluate boundary $Q_{\max}$ of that bracket for TAC. Compare all feasible TACs.
- [ ] **Never stop** at the lowest price bracket — always do the backward pass for boundary quantities.
- [ ] Decision is at the bracket with the **minimum TAC**, **not** necessarily the lowest unit price.

### 🔹 Single-Period Probabilistic Model (Newsvendor)

- [ ] **$C_u = p - c$** (underage cost = lost profit per stockout).
- [ ] **$C_o = c - s$** (overage cost = loss per leftover unit).
- [ ] **Critical Ratio** $\in (0,1)$: $\dfrac{C_u}{C_u + C_o}$.
- [ ] **Discrete:** Smallest $Q$ such that $F(Q) \ge \text{Critical Ratio}$.
- [ ] **Continuous Normal:** $Q^* = \mu + z \cdot \sigma$ where $z = \Phi^{-1}(\text{Critical Ratio})$.
- [ ] **Verification:** Compute $E[\pi]$ at $Q^*-1$, $Q^*$, $Q^*+1$ — maximum should be at $Q^*$.
- [ ] Service level = Critical Ratio; Stockout risk = $1 -$ Critical Ratio.

### 🔹 Conceptual Distinctions KTU Loves to Test

- [ ] All-Units vs Incremental Discounts (Q1 in Part A).
- [ ] When to use Poisson (discrete count data) vs Normal (continuous approx) for demand.
- [ ] Why EOQ is **not optimal** under discounts (because $C$ is not constant).
- [ ] Why the newsvendor model is **single-period** (no replenishment within the cycle).

### 🔹 Common Numerical Values to Remember

- [ ] $\Phi(0) = 0.5$, $\Phi(1.645) = 0.95$, $\Phi(1.96) = 0.975$.
- [ ] $L(0) \approx 0.3989$ (standard normal loss at mean).
- [ ] For symmetric costs ($C_u = C_o$), $CR = 0.5$, $z = 0$, $Q^* = \mu$.

### 🔹 KTU 2024 Mark Weightage Distribution

| Topic | Part A Probability | Part B Probability | Average Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| All-Units Quantity Discounts | 30% | 50% | ~6 marks |
| Newsvendor Discrete | 40% | 30% | ~4 marks |
| Newsvendor Continuous | 30% | 20% | ~4 marks |

> **Targeted Preparation:** Spend 60% of revision time on the **algorithm + numerical** for discounts, and 40% on **critical ratio mechanics + verification**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Reorder Point for Deterministic and Probabilistic Inventory System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT862 - OPERATIONAL RESEARCH AND INDUSTRIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Determination of Reorder Point for Deterministic and Probabilistic Inventory System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Reorder Point (ROP)**, also called the **Reorder Level (ROL)** or **Reorder Quantity Trigger Point**, is a critically engineered inventory threshold — measured in units of stock — at which a replenishment order must be initiated to a supplier so that the new consignment arrives *exactly* before the on-hand inventory is exhausted.

Mathematically, it represents the inventory position (on-hand stock + outstanding orders − backorders) at the moment a purchase order is released. The reorder point is fundamentally a function of:
- **Demand rate** (units consumed per unit time)
- **Lead time** (the time gap between placing and receiving an order)
- **Variability / Uncertainty** in either demand or lead time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Inventory Management):**
> Students must clearly distinguish between the **Deterministic Inventory System** (where demand and lead time are known constants) and the **Probabilistic Inventory System** (where demand and/or lead time follow a known probability distribution). The reorder point formula structurally differs between these two paradigms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bathroom Tissue Story"

Imagine you are at home and you know that your family uses **exactly 4 rolls of bathroom tissue per week** (deterministic — perfectly predictable). The local shop takes **2 days** to deliver after you place an order. The moment your stock drops to **(4 rolls/week × 2/7 week) ≈ 1.14 rolls**, you must call the shop. That threshold is the **Reorder Point**.

Now consider a more realistic scenario: the local kirana shop experiences *random* daily footfalls, so tissue consumption fluctuates. Sometimes you need 3 rolls in a week, sometimes 6. To be safe, you maintain a **buffer cushion** (called **Safety Stock**) on top of the average expected consumption. The new threshold becomes:

> **ROP = Expected consumption during lead time + Safety Buffer**

This cushion is what converts a deterministic problem into a probabilistic one.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board Examination Vocabulary)

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $D$ or $d$ | Demand rate | units/day, units/week |
| $L$ | Lead time | days, weeks |
| $Q$ | Order quantity (EOQ) | units |
| $\sigma_d$ | Standard deviation of daily demand | units |
| $\sigma_L$ | Standard deviation of lead time | days |
| $z$ | Standard normal variate for service level | dimensionless |
| $SS$ | Safety Stock | units |
| $TC$ | Total Cost | ₹/year |
| $SL$ | Service Level | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stock-out Probability vs. Cycle Service Level:**
> Cycle service level = 1 − Probability of stock-out per replenishment cycle. KTU questions typically state "service level = 95%" — this maps to a $z$ value of **1.645** (one-tailed standard normal). Common $z$ values to memorize:
> - 90% → $z = 1.28$
> - 95% → $z = 1.645$
> - 97.5% → $z = 1.96$
> - 99% → $z = 2.33$
> - 99.9% → $z = 3.09$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Behind Reorder Point Determination

The reorder point is essentially an *anticipatory trigger*. The reasoning unfolds in three crisp logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Consumption Trajectory:**
  Once the current stock is depleted at the rate of consumption, a *depletion curve* emerges. If demand is deterministic, this curve is a perfectly straight line. If demand is probabilistic, the curve zigzags around the mean.

- **Step 2 — Identify the Replenishment Lag:**
  The supplier does not deliver instantaneously. There is a *Lead Time* ($L$) during which the inventory keeps depleting. If we order *too late*, we run out (stock-out). If we order *too early*, we pile up excessive holding cost.

- **Step 3 — Match the Trigger to the Lead-Time Demand:**
  The reorder point must equal the *expected quantity that will be consumed during the lead time*, possibly plus a safety buffer if uncertainty exists. This is the *just-in-time* order release.

## 2.2 The "Why" — Engineering Utility of ROP

- **Prevents production halts** in manufacturing (raw material stock-out stops the assembly line).
- **Prevents lost sales** in retail (empty shelves mean lost customers).
- **Minimizes carrying costs** by avoiding over-ordering.
- **Forms the operational backbone** of MRP (Material Requirements Planning) and ERP systems in real industries.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for the ESE Board Exam**

| Case | Reorder Point Formula | Conditions for Validity |
|---|---|---|
| **Deterministic — Constant Demand & Constant Lead Time** | $ROP = d \times L$ | Demand rate is fixed, lead time is fixed |
| **Probabilistic — Variable Demand, Constant Lead Time** | $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Lead time fixed, demand normally distributed |
| **Probabilistic — Constant Demand, Variable Lead Time** | $ROP = d \cdot \bar{L} + z \cdot d \cdot \sigma_L$ | Demand fixed, lead time variable |
| **Probabilistic — Both Variable Demand & Variable Lead Time** | $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} + z \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2}\sigma_L^{2}}$ | Both stochastic (most general case) |
| **Safety Stock Formula (Probabilistic)** | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_L$ | Where $\sigma_L$ is the lead-time demand std. dev. |
| **Lead-Time Demand Std. Dev. (Demand Variable Only)** | $\sigma_L = \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Independent daily demands |
| **Lead-Time Demand Std. Dev. (Both Variable)** | $\sigma_L = \sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2}\sigma_L^{2}}$ | Variance additive when independent |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Variance Addition Principle:**
> When lead time and demand are both random and statistically *independent*, the variances add up. The lead-time demand is $X = d \cdot L$ (a product of two random variables), and its variance can be derived as:
> $$Var(X) = \bar{L} \cdot \sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2} \cdot \sigma_L^{2}$$
> This is a KTU-favorite formula appearing in 14-mark problems.

## 2.4 The Core "How" — Step-by-Step Logic

For a **Deterministic System**:
1. Identify constant demand rate $d$ (units/day).
2. Identify constant lead time $L$ (days).
3. Multiply: $ROP = d \times L$.
4. Optional sanity check: $ROP$ should be less than $EOQ$ for a feasible cycle.

For a **Probabilistic System**:
1. Identify $\bar{d}$, $\sigma_d$, $\bar{L}$, $\sigma_L$.
2. Compute expected lead-time demand: $\mu_L = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L}$.
3. Compute standard deviation of lead-time demand: $\sigma_L$ (using the correct variance formula).
4. Look up the $z$-score corresponding to the given service level.
5. Compute Safety Stock: $SS = z \cdot \sigma_L$.
6. Final ROP = $\mu_L + SS$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Deterministic Reorder Point

**Setup:** A firm consumes a known constant quantity $d$ units per day. The supplier delivers exactly after $L$ days from order placement. We want to find the inventory level $ROP$ that triggers an order so that the new stock arrives *just* as the old stock reaches zero.

Let $I(t)$ = on-hand inventory at time $t$.

At $t = 0$ (moment of order), inventory = $ROP$.

Between $t = 0$ and $t = L$, inventory depletes at rate $d$:

$$I(t) = ROP - d \cdot t, \quad \text{for } 0 \leq t \leq L$$

At $t = L$ (moment of delivery), inventory should be zero to avoid both shortage and over-stock:

$$I(L) = ROP - d \cdot L = 0$$

Solving for $ROP$:

$$ROP = d \cdot L$$

This is the canonical **deterministic reorder point formula**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Probabilistic Reorder Point (Variable Demand, Constant Lead Time)

**Setup:** Daily demand $D_i$ is a random variable with mean $\bar{d}$ and standard deviation $\sigma_d$. Lead time $L$ is constant.

Total demand over the lead time is:

$$X = D_1 + D_2 + D_3 + \ldots + D_L = \sum_{i=1}^{L} D_i$$

Since the demands are independent and identically distributed:

$$\text{Mean lead-time demand: } \mu_L = E[X] = L \cdot \bar{d}$$

For the variance, by independence:

$$\sigma_L^{2} = Var(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{L} Var(D_i) = L \cdot \sigma_d^{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_L = \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

The reorder point must cover the *expected* consumption during lead time plus a *safety buffer* proportional to the standard deviation:

$$ROP = \mu_L + z \cdot \sigma_L = L \cdot \bar{d} + z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Probabilistic Reorder Point (Both Demand and Lead Time Variable)

**Setup:** Daily demand has mean $\bar{d}$ and std. dev. $\sigma_d$. Lead time is random with mean $\bar{L}$ and std. dev. $\sigma_L$. Assume independence.

Total lead-time demand is the random product:

$$X = D \cdot L$$

To compute $E[X]$ and $Var(X)$ for two independent random variables, we use the standard product-moment identities:

$$E[D \cdot L] = E[D] \cdot E[L] = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L}$$

For variance of a product of independent variables:

$$Var(D \cdot L) = E[D]^{2} \cdot Var(L) + E[L]^{2} \cdot Var(D) + Var(D) \cdot Var(L)$$

In KTU-board practice, the higher-order term $Var(D) \cdot Var(L)$ is typically negligible when both $\sigma_d \ll \bar{d}$ and $\sigma_L \ll \bar{L}$. Hence the **practical working formula** used:

$$\sigma_{DL} = \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2} \cdot \sigma_L^{2}}$$

And the reorder point:

$$ROP = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} + z \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2} \cdot \sigma_L^{2}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Board Pattern)

### Example 1 — Deterministic Case (3-mark type)

> **Problem:** A company uses 200 units of a raw material per day. The supplier takes 6 days to deliver. Find the reorder point.

**Solution:**

Given: $d = 200$ units/day, $L = 6$ days.

$$ROP = d \times L = 200 \times 6 = 1200 \text{ units}$$

The company should place a new order when the inventory drops to **1200 units**.

### Example 2 — Probabilistic Case (14-mark type)

> **Problem:** A retail store sells an item with average daily demand of 50 units with a standard deviation of 10 units. The lead time is constant at 8 days. The store wants to maintain a 95% service level. Compute the reorder point and safety stock. ($z = 1.645$ for 95% service)

**Solution:**

Given: $\bar{d} = 50$ units/day, $\sigma_d = 10$ units, $L = 8$ days, $z = 1.645$.

**Step 1:** Mean lead-time demand:

$$\mu_L = \bar{d} \cdot L = 50 \times 8 = 400 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2:** Standard deviation of lead-time demand:

$$\sigma_L = \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L} = 10 \cdot \sqrt{8} = 10 \cdot 2.8284 = 28.284 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3:** Safety stock:

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_L = 1.645 \times 28.284 = 46.53 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4:** Reorder point:

$$ROP = \mu_L + SS = 400 + 46.53 = 446.53 \approx 447 \text{ units}$$

**Answer:** Reorder point = **447 units**, Safety Stock = **47 units**.

### Example 3 — Both Variable (Advanced 14-mark)

> **Problem:** Demand is normally distributed with mean 100 units/day ($\sigma_d = 20$). Lead time has mean 10 days ($\sigma_L = 2$ days). Service level = 98% ($z = 2.05$). Find ROP.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Mean lead-time demand:

$$\mu_L = 100 \times 10 = 1000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2:** Combined standard deviation:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2}\sigma_L^{2}} = \sqrt{10 \cdot 400 + 10000 \cdot 4}$$

$$= \sqrt{4000 + 40000} = \sqrt{44000} = 209.76 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3:** Safety stock:

$$SS = 2.05 \times 209.76 = 430.01 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4:** Reorder point:

$$ROP = 1000 + 430.01 = 1430.01 \approx 1431 \text{ units}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inventory Profile — Deterministic System

The deterministic inventory profile shows a classic **sawtooth wave**. Inventory depletes linearly with a slope of $-d$, drops to the reorder point, and instantly jumps back up by the order quantity $Q$ upon delivery.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inventory at Q units] -->|Steady depletion at rate d| B[Inventory reaches ROP]
    B -->|Order placed| C[Lead time L elapses]
    C -->|New stock arrives Q units| D[Inventory jumps back to Q]
    D -->|Cycle repeats| A
```

**Node Legend (as rendered in the diagram above):**
- **A** = Peak inventory level (right after delivery) = $Q$ units
- **B** = Reorder Point (ROP) = $d \cdot L$
- **C** = Lead-time gap (waiting period of $L$ days)
- **D** = Instantaneous stock replenishment by $Q$ units

## 4.2 Inventory Profile — Probabilistic System

In a probabilistic setting, the depletion curve is *noisy*. The reorder point is set above the expected lead-time demand to absorb the demand surges.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[On-hand inventory at Q] --> P2[Random fluctuations in daily demand]
    P2 --> P3[Inventory declines to Expected LT Demand]
    P3 --> P4[Add Safety Stock cushion]
    P4 --> P5[Reorder Point ROP reached]
    P5 --> P6[Place order to supplier]
    P6 --> P7[Wait for lead time L]
    P7 --> P8{Does new stock arrive before stock-out?}
    P8 -->|Yes - service level met| P9[Replenishment by Q units]
    P8 -->|No - rare stock-out event| P10[Lost sales or backorder]
    P9 --> P1
    P10 --> P1
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ROP Decision System

The reorder point operates inside a continuous **monitoring-control-actuation loop** in real ERP systems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inventory Database] -->|Real-time stock level| B[Monitoring Module]
    B -->|Current stock value| C{Comparison: Stock vs ROP}
    D[Demand Forecasting Engine] -->|Mean and std dev of demand| C
    E[Lead Time Tracker] -->|Mean and std dev of LT| C
    C -->|Stock greater than ROP| F[Hold Position - No Action]
    C -->|Stock less or equal to ROP| G[Trigger Purchase Order]
    G -->|Purchase order to supplier| H[Supplier Acknowledgement]
    H -->|Goods receipt after L days| A
```

## 4.4 Comparative Summary Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Inventory Management System] --> Q1{Is demand constant?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Det[Deterministic Sub-system]
    Q1 -->|No| Prob[Probabilistic Sub-system]
    Det --> F1[ROP equals d times L]
    Prob --> Q2{Is lead time constant?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| F2[ROP equals d-bar times L plus z times sigma-d times sqrt L]
    Q2 -->|No| F3[ROP equals d-bar times L-bar plus z times sqrt variance sum]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the term *Reorder Point* in inventory management. State the formula for the reorder point under deterministic conditions with constant demand and constant lead time.

**Model Answer:**

The **Reorder Point (ROP)** is the inventory level at which a new replenishment order must be triggered so that the fresh stock arrives just as the existing stock is exhausted, thereby preventing stock-outs without incurring excess holding cost.

Under **deterministic conditions** — i.e., when demand rate ($d$) and lead time ($L$) are both known and constant — the reorder point is given by:

$$ROP = d \times L$$

where $d$ is the demand rate in units per unit time and $L$ is the lead time in the same time units.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** What is *Safety Stock*? Why is it required in a probabilistic inventory system?

**Model Answer:**

**Safety Stock (SS)** is an additional quantity of inventory held as a buffer against unforeseen fluctuations in demand and/or lead time, ensuring that the probability of stock-out is kept below a pre-defined threshold (the service level).

In a **probabilistic inventory system**, demand and/or lead time are random variables. The reorder point must therefore be set *above* the expected lead-time demand to absorb variability. The required safety stock is calculated as:

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_L$$

where $z$ is the standard normal variate corresponding to the desired service level, and $\sigma_L$ is the standard deviation of demand during the lead time.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module 4 typically allows the student an internal choice between two long-answer questions. Both options below are framed in the exact KTU style with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**A company experiences an average daily demand of 80 units for a particular component. The standard deviation of daily demand is 12 units. The lead time is constant at 9 days. The management wants to maintain a service level of 97.5% ($z = 1.96$).**

**(a)** Calculate the reorder point and the safety stock for this probabilistic inventory system. (7 marks)

**(b)** If the lead time becomes variable with a mean of 9 days and a standard deviation of 2 days (in addition to the variable demand above), recompute the reorder point and safety stock. Comment on the change. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — Solution

**Given:** $\bar{d} = 80$ units/day, $\sigma_d = 12$ units, $L = 9$ days (constant), $z = 1.96$.

**Step 1:** Mean lead-time demand:

$$\mu_L = \bar{d} \times L = 80 \times 9 = 720 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2:** Standard deviation of lead-time demand (constant $L$):

$$\sigma_L = \sigma_d \times \sqrt{L} = 12 \times \sqrt{9} = 12 \times 3 = 36 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3:** Safety Stock:

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_L = 1.96 \times 36 = 70.56 \approx 70.56 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4:** Reorder Point:

$$ROP = \mu_L + SS = 720 + 70.56 = 790.56 \approx 791 \text{ units}$$

**Answer (a):** Safety Stock = **70.56 units**, ROP = **791 units**.

##### Part (b) — Solution

**Given (new):** $\bar{d} = 80$ units/day, $\sigma_d = 12$ units, $\bar{L} = 9$ days, $\sigma_L = 2$ days, $z = 1.96$.

**Step 1:** Mean lead-time demand (unchanged):

$$\mu_L = \bar{d} \times \bar{L} = 80 \times 9 = 720 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2:** Combined standard deviation (both random):

$$\sigma_{combined} = \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2} \cdot \sigma_L^{2}}$$

$$= \sqrt{9 \times 144 + 6400 \times 4}$$

$$= \sqrt{1296 + 25600}$$

$$= \sqrt{26896} = 164.0 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3:** Safety Stock:

$$SS = 1.96 \times 164.0 = 321.44 \approx 321.44 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4:** Reorder Point:

$$ROP = 720 + 321.44 = 1041.44 \approx 1042 \text{ units}$$

**Comment on the change:**
- The safety stock increased from **70.56 units → 321.44 units** (an increase of ~355%).
- The reorder point increased from **791 → 1042 units** (an increase of ~31.7%).
- This dramatic jump occurs because the *combined* uncertainty (demand + lead time) is much larger than demand uncertainty alone. The variance addition principle effectively squares the impact.

**Answer (b):** Safety Stock = **321.44 units**, ROP = **1042 units**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**A manufacturing unit consumes 500 units of a particular bolt per day at a constant deterministic rate. The supplier has a fixed lead time of 7 days. The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) for this item is 5000 units.**

**(a)** Compute the reorder point and the maximum inventory level. Also determine the average inventory. (7 marks)

**(b)** If the demand becomes probabilistic with mean 500 units/day and standard deviation 25 units/day, while the lead time becomes probabilistic with mean 7 days and standard deviation 1 day, calculate the new reorder point at a 95% service level ($z = 1.645$). Compare the two scenarios. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Solution

**Given (deterministic):** $d = 500$ units/day, $L = 7$ days, $Q = 5000$ units.

**Step 1:** Reorder Point:

$$ROP = d \times L = 500 \times 7 = 3500 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2:** Maximum inventory level (occurs right after delivery):

$$I_{max} = Q = 5000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3:** Minimum inventory level (occurs right before next delivery):

$$I_{min} = ROP - d \times L = 3500 - 3500 = 0 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4:** Average inventory (for a sawtooth pattern):

$$I_{avg} = \frac{I_{max} + I_{min}}{2} = \frac{5000 + 0}{2} = 2500 \text{ units}$$

**Answer (a):** ROP = **3500 units**, $I_{max}$ = **5000 units**, $I_{avg}$ = **2500 units**.

##### Part (b) — Solution

**Given (probabilistic):** $\bar{d} = 500$ units/day, $\sigma_d = 25$ units/day, $\bar{L} = 7$ days, $\sigma_L = 1$ day, $z = 1.645$.

**Step 1:** Mean lead-time demand:

$$\mu_L = \bar{d} \times \bar{L} = 500 \times 7 = 3500 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2:** Combined standard deviation:

$$\sigma_{combined} = \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2} \cdot \sigma_L^{2}}$$

$$= \sqrt{7 \times 625 + 250000 \times 1}$$

$$= \sqrt{4375 + 250000}$$

$$= \sqrt{254375} = 504.36 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3:** Safety Stock:

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_{combined} = 1.645 \times 504.36 = 829.67 \approx 829.67 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4:** Reorder Point:

$$ROP = 3500 + 829.67 = 4329.67 \approx 4330 \text{ units}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Deterministic | Probabilistic | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reorder Point | 3500 units | 4330 units | +23.7% |
| Safety Stock | 0 units | 830 units | New addition |
| Stock-out risk | Zero | 5% | New exposure |

**Answer (b):** New ROP = **4330 units**. The probabilistic system requires an additional 830 units as safety stock, pushing the reorder point up by ~24% compared to the deterministic case.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 ROP Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the square root on lead time** — A frequent blunder is writing $\sigma_L = \sigma_d \cdot L$ instead of $\sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$. The square root is *non-negotiable* because variances, not standard deviations, add linearly over independent days.
>
> 2. **Mixing up the two variance formulas** — When *only* lead time is variable, students incorrectly apply the "both variable" formula $\sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_d^2 + \bar{d}^2\sigma_L^2}$. Use the simpler $d \cdot \sigma_L$ in that case.
>
> 3. **Wrong $z$-value lookup** — The question may say "98% service level". Students must use $z = 2.05$ (not 1.96, which is 97.5%). A single $z$-value error typically costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Not converting units consistently** — If demand is in units/week and lead time is in days, students must convert either demand to units/day or lead time to weeks. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for unit inconsistency.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Safety Stock in the final answer** — Some students compute $SS$ but forget to add it to the mean lead-time demand. The ROP *always* includes the safety stock in a probabilistic system.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the EOQ feasibility check** — In a 14-mark question, the examiner expects the student to verify that $ROP < EOQ$ for a viable cycle. Skipping this check can cost 1 mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Reorder Point**

- **Deterministic ROP:** $ROP = d \times L$ — simplest, used in textbooks.
- **Probabilistic ROP (Demand Variable Only):** $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$.
- **Probabilistic ROP (Lead Time Variable Only):** $ROP = d \cdot \bar{L} + z \cdot d \cdot \sigma_L$.
- **Probabilistic ROP (Both Variable):** $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} + z \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2}\sigma_L^{2}}$.
- **Safety Stock** is *zero* in deterministic systems and *positive* in probabilistic systems.
- **Lead-time demand variance** equals $L \cdot \sigma_d^2$ when lead time is constant.
- **Variances add** when demand and lead time are independent random variables.
- **Standard $z$-values** to memorize: 1.28 (90%), 1.645 (95%), 1.96 (97.5%), 2.33 (99%).
- **Service level** is the *probability* of not stocking out during a cycle.
- **Maximum inventory** = $Q$ (just after delivery) in a deterministic sawtooth model.
- **Average inventory** = $Q/2$ for deterministic EOQ model.
- **Feasibility check:** $ROP < EOQ$ must hold; otherwise the system is infeasible.
- The reorder point is an **inventory position trigger**, not a quantity to order.
- The *quantity to order* at the ROP is the EOQ (or a multiple thereof).
- **Real-world applications:** Production planning, hospital pharmacy stocks, spare parts inventory, retail replenishment.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Determination of Reorder Point for Deterministic and Probabilistic Inventory System**
